examples.question: Is Hermes vigorous?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether Hermes possesses the quality of vigor. The goal is to trace a chain of implications from the facts and rules provided until we can assert Hermes is vigorous.

The first premise establishes a direct link between being strategic and being commanding for Hermes. This conditional will be useful once we know Hermes is strategic.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule that if Hermes is strategic, then Hermes is commanding.</text>
<logic>commanding(hermes) :- strategic(hermes)</logic>
</step>

The second premise tells us that resourcefulness guarantees vigor for Hermes. It will become relevant after we can show Hermes is resourceful.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule that if Hermes is resourceful, then Hermes is vigorous.</text>
<logic>vigorous(hermes) :- resourceful(hermes)</logic>
</step>

The third premise connects commanding to amicability, allowing us to move from a command role to a friendly demeanor. This bridge will be needed after we derive Hermes's commanding status.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule that if Hermes is commanding, then Hermes is amicable.</text>
<logic>amicable(hermes) :- commanding(hermes)</logic>
</step>

The fourth premise states that an amicable Hermes is reposed, giving us a way to obtain a rested condition. It will be applied once we have established Hermes as amicable.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The rule that if Hermes is amicable, then Hermes is reposed.</text>
<logic>reposed(hermes) :- amicable(hermes)</logic>
</step>

The fifth premise provides a contrapositive style link: a rigorous Hermes cannot be reposed. This will help us eliminate the possibility of Hermes being rigorous later on.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The rule that if Hermes is rigorous, then Hermes is not reposed.</text>
<logic>~reposed(hermes) :- rigorous(hermes)</logic>
</step>

The sixth premise offers a disjunction: Hermes must be either rigorous or resourceful. This binary choice will become decisive after we rule out one of the options.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>The statement that Hermes is either rigorous or resourceful.</text>
<logic>rigorous(hermes) v resourceful(hermes)</logic>
</step>

The seventh premise supplies the concrete fact that Hermes is strategic. This fact is the starting point for the forward reasoning chain.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>The fact that Hermes is strategic.</text>
<logic>strategic(hermes)</logic>
</step>

Given Hermes is strategic (from the fact) and the rule that strategic implies commanding, we can confidently assert Hermes is commanding. This is a straightforward application of modus ponens.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,1">
<text>From Hermes being strategic and the first rule we infer that Hermes is commanding.</text>
<logic>commanding(hermes)</logic>
</step>

Now that Hermes is commanding, the rule linking commanding to amicability allows us to conclude Hermes is amicable. Again, modus ponens lets us move from the antecedent to the consequent.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,3">
<text>Using Hermes's new status as commanding and the third rule, we deduce that Hermes is amicable.</text>
<logic>amicable(hermes)</logic>
</step>

With Hermes now amicable, the rule that amicable leads to being reposed lets us infer Hermes is reposed. This continues the chain of direct implications.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,4">
<text>Given Hermes is amicable, the fourth rule lets us conclude that Hermes is reposed.</text>
<logic>reposed(hermes)</logic>
</step>

We have Hermes reposed, but the rule about rigorous Hermes says a rigorous Hermes would not be reposed. Since Hermes is reposed, the only way to avoid contradiction is to assert that Hermes is not rigorous. This is a classic use of modus tollens.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,5">
<text>Since Hermes is reposed and the fifth rule says a rigorous Hermes would not be reposed, we obtain that Hermes cannot be rigorous.</text>
<logic>~rigorous(hermes)</logic>
</step>

We have established that Hermes is not rigorous, yet the disjunction tells us he must be either rigorous or resourceful. Eliminating the rigorous alternative forces the conclusion that Hermes is resourceful. This is a standard disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,6">
<text>Because Hermes is not rigorous and we know he is either rigorous or resourceful, the only remaining possibility is that Hermes is resourceful.</text>
<logic>resourceful(hermes)</logic>
</step>

Having shown Hermes is resourceful, the second rule (resourceful implies vigorous) lets us deduce Hermes is vigorous. This final application of modus ponens yields the property we were asked to verify.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,2">
<text>Now that Hermes is resourceful, the second rule yields that Hermes is vigorous.</text>
<logic>vigorous(hermes)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Hermes is vigorous, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: Sam is athletic. Whenever something is athletic, it is logical, so Sam is logical. If Sam is logical, then Sam is attentive. Anything that is attentive is also efficient, which makes Sam efficient. If something is responsible, then it is insightful, and any experienced thing is responsible, so an experienced Sam would be responsible and consequently insightful. Moreover, if Sam were knowledgeable, then Sam would not be efficient. Finally, Sam is either knowledgeable or experienced (or possibly both).
examples.question: Is Sam adaptable?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide if Sam has the property adaptable. The knowledge base contains several attributes of Sam and rules that connect those attributes, but none of the rules mention adaptability directly. Therefore our task is to see whether the existing chain of implications can reach adaptability or its negation.

I need to determine whether Sam is adaptable can be derived from the given premises.

This premise links logicalness to attentiveness. If we can establish that Sam is logical, we will be able to infer that Sam is attentive, which may be useful for later steps. It is therefore a potential stepping stone in a longer derivation.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Sam is logical then Sam is attentive.</text>
<logic>attentive(sam) :- logical(sam)</logic>
</step>

Here we are told that attentiveness leads to efficiency. Should we manage to prove Sam is attentive, this rule will let us claim Sam is efficient. Knowing Sam's efficiency could later interact with other rules, such as the one about knowledgeability.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Sam is attentive then Sam is efficient.</text>
<logic>efficient(sam) :- attentive(sam)</logic>
</step>

This rule connects responsibility with insightfulness. If Sam were shown to be responsible, we could conclude Sam is insightful. Insightfulness is another attribute that might be relevant if other rules referenced it, though none do in this problem.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Sam is responsible then Sam is insightful.</text>
<logic>insightful(sam) :- responsible(sam)</logic>
</step>

The fourth premise tells us that athleticism guarantees logicalness. Since we already have the fact that Sam is athletic, this rule immediately gives us Sam is logical, which activates PREMISE_1.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Sam is athletic then Sam is logical.</text>
<logic>logical(sam) :- athletic(sam)</logic>
</step>

According to this rule, being experienced makes one responsible. If we could prove Sam is experienced, we would obtain responsibility and then, via PREMISE_3, insightfulness. The disjunction in the context gives us a choice between knowledgeable or experienced, but does not settle which holds.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Sam is experienced then Sam is responsible.</text>
<logic>responsible(sam) :- experienced(sam)</logic>
</step>

This premise says that knowledgeability forces the negation of efficiency. Should we be able to infer Sam is knowledgeable, we would learn that Sam is not efficient, which would conflict with the efficiency we could derive from PREMISE_2. The disjunction again leaves this uncertain.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Sam is knowledgeable then Sam is not efficient.</text>
<logic>~efficient(sam) :- knowledgeable(sam)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Sam is athletic is the starting point for the chain. Using PREMISE_4 we get Sam is logical, which then triggers PREMISE_1 and so on. However, none of these deductions mention adaptability.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>The fact tells us that Sam is athletic.</text>
<logic>athletic(sam)</logic>
</step>

<step n="8" type="derived">
<text>Searching for rules that conclude Sam is adaptable: no rule or disjunction has "adaptable" as a consequent.</text>
<logic>unreachable(adaptable(sam))</logic>
</step>

<step n="9" type="derived">
<text>Similarly, no rule concludes Sam is not adaptable.</text>
<logic>unreachable(~adaptable(sam))</logic>
</step>

<step n="10" type="derived">
<text>Neither Sam is adaptable nor Sam is not adaptable can be derived from the given premises. The answer is Unknown.</text>
<logic>unknown(adaptable(sam))</logic>
</step>

</think>
Answer: Unknown

examples.context: In the ancient world, the city of Troy is noted as ab_courageous and it is also described as fit. Harley, a warrior from the same era, is known to be generous. The scholars assert that any generous individual is also fit; thus generosity guarantees fitness. Furthermore, a default principle holds that if Harley is fit and there is no evidence that Harley is ab_courageous, then Harley can be regarded as courageous. No other alternatives or contradictions are mentioned.
examples.question: Is Harley not courageous?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether Harley lacks the quality of courage. To answer this, we must examine the facts and rules that connect generosity, fitness, and courage for Harley.

The first premise provides a direct link between generosity and fitness: whenever someone is generous, they are also fit. This relationship will let us turn Harley's known generosity into a statement about his fitness.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that any generous individual is also fit.</text>
<logic>fit(harley) :- generous(harley)</logic>
</step>

The second premise is a default rule that activates when Harley is fit and there is no evidence that he is ab_courageous. It tells us that under those conditions Harley can be considered courageous. This rule is crucial for deriving courage later on.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The default rule says that if Harley is fit and there is no proof that Harley is ab_courageous, then Harley is courageous.</text>
<logic>courageous(harley) :- fit(harley), not ab_courageous(harley)</logic>
</step>

The third premise records that Troy is ab_courageous. While this fact does not involve Harley directly, it establishes that the predicate ab_courageous is true for at least one individual, which matters for the default's negation condition.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>It is given that Troy possesses the property ab_courageous.</text>
<logic>ab_courageous(troy)</logic>
</step>

The fourth premise states that Troy is fit. Like the previous fact, it does not concern Harley, but it completes the background knowledge about the world described in the context.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The fact also tells us that Troy is fit.</text>
<logic>fit(troy)</logic>
</step>

The fifth premise tells us that Harley is generous. This is the starting point for our chain of reasoning, because the rule from premise 1 will let us infer something new about Harley from this fact.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Harley is described as generous.</text>
<logic>generous(harley)</logic>
</step>

Applying the rule from premise 1 to the fact that Harley is generous, we conclude that Harley must be fit. This step is a straightforward use of modus ponens: generosity implies fitness, and Harley satisfies the antecedent.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>From Harley's generosity and the rule that generosity implies fitness, we infer that Harley is fit.</text>
<logic>fit(harley)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established Harley's fitness, we check the default rule from premise 2. The rule requires Harley to be fit (which we have) and that there be no evidence that Harley is ab_courageous. Since the knowledge base contains no statement about Harley being ab_courageous, the negation‑as‑failure condition is satisfied, allowing us to infer that Harley is courageous.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">
<text>Since we now know Harley is fit and we have no information that Harley is ab_courageous, the default rule allows us to conclude that Harley is courageous.</text>
<logic>courageous(harley)</logic>
</step>

We derived Harley is courageous, so the conclusion Harley is not courageous is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: In the pottery workshop, Cyrus is known to be reflective. Whenever someone is systematic, they are compassionate. If a person is not judicious, then they are grateful. Being reflective guarantees that the person is persistent. If a person is judicious, then they are not compassionate. Moreover, either Cyrus is not persistent or he is systematic.
examples.question: Is Cyrus not grateful?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to decide whether the claim that Cyrus is not grateful holds. The goal is to see if the premises force Cyrus to be grateful, which would make the negation false.

This rule tells us that systematic behavior leads to compassion. It will become useful once we can establish that Cyrus is systematic later in the proof.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Cyrus is systematic, then he is compassionate.</text>
<logic>compassionate(cyrus) :- systematic(cyrus)</logic>
</step>

Here we have a direct link from not being judicious to being grateful. If we can show Cyrus is not judicious, this rule will give us the desired grateful conclusion.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Cyrus is not judicious, then he is grateful.</text>
<logic>grateful(cyrus) :- ~judicious(cyrus)</logic>
</step>

The third rule connects being reflective with being persistent. Since we already know Cyrus is reflective, this rule allows us to derive persistence.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Cyrus is reflective, then he is persistent.</text>
<logic>persistent(cyrus) :- reflective(cyrus)</logic>
</step>

This conditional says that judiciousness forces a lack of compassion. It will later be used in a contrapositive style to infer that Cyrus cannot be judicious once we have compassion.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If Cyrus is judicious, then he is not compassionate.</text>
<logic>~compassionate(cyrus) :- judicious(cyrus)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction offers two possibilities: either Cyrus is not persistent or he is systematic. Combined with other information, it will let us pick the systematic alternative.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Either Cyrus is not persistent or he is systematic.</text>
<logic>~persistent(cyrus) v systematic(cyrus)</logic>
</step>

The fact states directly that Cyrus possesses the reflective quality, providing the starting point for the chain of deductions.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Cyrus is reflective.</text>
<logic>reflective(cyrus)</logic>
</step>

Because Cyrus is reflective (Premise 6) and the rule in Premise 3 says reflective implies persistent, we can apply modus ponens to obtain that Cyrus is persistent. This gives us a concrete fact to work with in the next step.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">
<text>From Cyrus being reflective we infer that he is persistent.</text>
<logic>persistent(cyrus)</logic>
</step>

We now have persistent(cyrus) from Step 7, and the disjunction in Premise 5 says either not persistent or systematic. Since persistent is true, the first alternative of the disjunction is false, forcing the second alternative to be true; thus Cyrus must be systematic. This is a classic use of disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,5">
<text>Since Cyrus is persistent and either not persistent or systematic holds, we conclude that he must be systematic.</text>
<logic>systematic(cyrus)</logic>
</step>

Having established systematic(cyrus) in Step 8, we can invoke the rule from Premise 1 (systematic → compassionate). Modus ponens gives us compassionate(cyrus), adding another useful property to the argument.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,1">
<text>Given that Cyrus is systematic, we deduce that he is compassionate.</text>
<logic>compassionate(cyrus)</logic>
</step>

We now possess compassionate(cyrus) and the rule in Premise 4 that judiciousness would imply not compassionate. Using modus tollens, if judiciousness were true, compassionate would be false, which contradicts our derived compassion. Therefore Cyrus cannot be judicious, yielding ~judicious(cyrus).
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,4">
<text>Because Cyrus is compassionate and if someone is judicious they would be not compassionate, we infer that Cyrus cannot be judicious.</text>
<logic>~judicious(cyrus)</logic>
</step>

With ~judicious(cyrus) in hand, the rule from Premise 2 (not judicious → grateful) lets us apply modus ponens once more, giving us grateful(cyrus). This directly opposes the query’s negation.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,2">
<text>From Cyrus not being judicious, the rule that non‑judicious implies grateful gives us that Cyrus is grateful.</text>
<logic>grateful(cyrus)</logic>
</step>

We derived Cyrus is grateful, so the conclusion Cyrus is not grateful is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: George works at the library and is known to be capable. Whenever someone is capable, they are experienced. Whenever someone is experienced, they become systematic. Whenever someone is systematic, they are motivated. If a person is eloquent, then they are not motivated. If a person is confident, then they are brilliant. If a person is observant, then they are confident. If a person is brilliant, then they are reliable. George is either eloquent or observant, and either not reliable or focused.
examples.question: Is George focused?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to determine whether George possesses the property of being focused. The context gives us a network of conditional statements and a couple of alternatives that together allow us to chain from the known fact that George is capable to the desired conclusion.

<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If George is confident then George is brilliant.</text>
<logic>brilliant(george) :- confident(george)</logic>
</step>

<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If George is observant then George is confident.</text>
<logic>confident(george) :- observant(george)</logic>
</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If George is capable then George is experienced.</text>
<logic>experienced(george) :- capable(george)</logic>
</step>

<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If George is systematic then George is motivated.</text>
<logic>motivated(george) :- systematic(george)</logic>
</step>

<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If George is brilliant then George is reliable.</text>
<logic>reliable(george) :- brilliant(george)</logic>
</step>

<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>If George is experienced then George is systematic.</text>
<logic>systematic(george) :- experienced(george)</logic>
</step>

<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>If George is eloquent then George is not motivated.</text>
<logic>~motivated(george) :- eloquent(george)</logic>
</step>

<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>George is eloquent or George is observant.</text>
<logic>eloquent(george) v observant(george)</logic>
</step>

<step n="9" type="premise">
<text>Either George is not reliable or George is focused.</text>
<logic>~reliable(george) v focused(george)</logic>
</step>

<step n="10" type="premise">
<text>George is capable.</text>
<logic>capable(george)</logic>
</step>

We start with the explicit fact that George is capable. The rule "If someone is capable then they are experienced" lets us immediately upgrade this information, giving us that George is experienced. This is a straightforward application of the rule to the known fact.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,3">
<text>From George being capable and the rule that capable implies experienced, we infer that George is experienced.</text>
<logic>experienced(george)</logic>
</step>

Having established that George is experienced, we can use the rule "If someone is experienced then they are systematic". By applying this rule to the newly derived experienced status, we obtain that George is systematic. This step extends the chain one link further.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,6">
<text>Because George is experienced and experienced implies systematic, we infer that George is systematic.</text>
<logic>systematic(george)</logic>
</step>

Now that George is systematic, the rule "If someone is systematic then they are motivated" comes into play. Applying it yields that George is motivated. At this point we have moved from the original capability all the way to motivation.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,4">
<text>Given that George is systematic and systematic implies motivated, we infer that George is motivated.</text>
<logic>motivated(george)</logic>
</step>

The rule "If someone is eloquent then they are not motivated" tells us that being eloquent would contradict the motivated status we just derived. Since George is motivated, he cannot be eloquent; otherwise we would have both motivated and not motivated. Therefore we conclude that George is not eloquent.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,7">
<text>Since George is motivated and eloquent would make him not motivated, we conclude that George cannot be eloquent; thus George is not eloquent.</text>
<logic>~eloquent(george)</logic>
</step>

The context also says that George is either eloquent or observant. Because we have just shown he is not eloquent, the only way for the disjunction to hold is for George to be observant. This reasoning eliminates one option and forces the other.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,8">
<text>From the fact that George is not eloquent and the disjunction that George is eloquent or observant, we deduce that George must be observant.</text>
<logic>observant(george)</logic>
</step>

With George established as observant, we can invoke the rule "If someone is observant then they are confident". This gives us that George is confident, extending our chain toward brilliance.
<step n="16" type="derived" from="15,2">
<text>Because George is observant and observant implies confident, we derive that George is confident.</text>
<logic>confident(george)</logic>
</step>

Confidence leads to brilliance via the rule "If someone is confident then they are brilliant". Applying this to George's confidence yields that George is brilliant.
<step n="17" type="derived" from="16,1">
<text>With George confident and confidence implying brilliance, we obtain that George is brilliant.</text>
<logic>brilliant(george)</logic>
</step>

Brilliance, in turn, triggers reliability through the rule "If someone is brilliant then they are reliable". Consequently, George becomes reliable.
<step n="18" type="derived" from="17,5">
<text>Since George is brilliant and brilliance leads to reliability, we conclude that George is reliable.</text>
<logic>reliable(george)</logic>
</step>

Finally, we have the disjunction "Either George is not reliable or George is focused". Since we have just proved that George is reliable, the left side of the disjunction is false, forcing the right side to be true. Thus George must be focused.
<step n="19" type="derived" from="18,9">
<text>From George being reliable and the disjunction that either George is not reliable or focused, we infer that George is focused.</text>
<logic>focused(george)</logic>
</step>

Therefore George is focused, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: Patient_nico is neutral, and patient_winter is also neutral. Any patient that is neutral is experienced. Any patient that is experienced is diligent. If a patient is well prepared, then that patient is not diligent. These statements capture all the facts and rules that will be used in the reasoning process.
examples.question: Can we conclude that patient_winter is not well prepared?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are given information about two patients, their neutrality, and how neutrality, experience, diligence, and preparation relate. The goal is to determine whether patient_winter can be classified as well prepared.

The first rule tells us that neutrality guarantees experience. This connection will let us move from a neutral fact to an experienced fact later in the proof.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule that any neutral patient is experienced.</text>
<logic>experienced(patient_winter) :- neutral(patient_winter)</logic>
</step>

The second rule states that experience guarantees diligence. Once we have established experience for a patient, we can immediately infer diligence, which is crucial for the final contradiction.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule that any experienced patient is diligent.</text>
<logic>diligent(patient_winter) :- experienced(patient_winter)</logic>
</step>

The third rule provides a negative link: being well prepared forces a patient to lack diligence. This rule will be used to show that if a patient were well prepared, they could not be diligent, creating a conflict with earlier deductions.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule that if a patient is well prepared, then they are not diligent.</text>
<logic>~diligent(patient_winter) :- well_prepared(patient_winter)</logic>
</step>

Patient_nico being neutral is a straightforward fact, but it does not directly affect the query about patient_winter. It is included to reflect the full set of given information.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Patient_nico is neutral.</text>
<logic>neutral(patient_nico)</logic>
</step>

Patient_winter being neutral is the key factual premise that activates the first rule. It allows us to start the chain of implications that will lead to the final conclusion.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Patient_winter is neutral.</text>
<logic>neutral(patient_winter)</logic>
</step>

Because patient_winter is neutral and the rule says every neutral patient is experienced, we can conclude that patient_winter is experienced. This step translates the generic rule into a concrete property for the patient in question.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>From patient_winter being neutral and the rule that neutral implies experienced, we infer patient_winter is experienced.</text>
<logic>experienced(patient_winter)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know patient_winter is experienced, we apply the second rule, which tells us that experience entails diligence. Hence, patient_winter is diligent, giving us a positive property that will later clash with the well‑prepared rule.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">
<text>Using patient_winter's experienced status and the rule that experienced implies diligent, we infer patient_winter is diligent.</text>
<logic>diligent(patient_winter)</logic>
</step>

We have patient_winter being diligent, but the third rule says that if a patient were well prepared, they would be not diligent. Since patient_winter is already diligent, the only way to avoid a contradiction is to infer that patient_winter cannot be well prepared. This uses the incompatibility between diligence and the consequence of being well prepared.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,3">
<text>Since patient_winter is diligent and the rule that well prepared would make a patient not diligent, we conclude patient_winter cannot be well prepared.</text>
<logic>~well_prepared(patient_winter)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Patient_winter is not well prepared, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: Sarah is inventive. Whenever Sarah is skillful, she is calm. If Sarah is inventive, then she is responsible. If Sarah is patient, then she is not calm. Either Sarah is not responsible or she is skillful.
examples.question: Is Sarah patient?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether Sarah can be considered patient. To answer, we will trace the logical consequences of the given facts and rules until we can either confirm or deny the patient property.

The first rule tells us that skillfulness guarantees calmness. This will be useful later because if we can establish that Sarah is skillful, we will immediately obtain that she is calm, which interacts with the patient rule.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Sarah is skillful, then she is calm.</text>
<logic>calm(sarah) :- skillful(sarah)</logic>
</step>

The second rule links inventiveness to responsibility. Since we already know Sarah is inventive, this rule will let us derive that she is responsible, a fact that will be needed for the disjunction.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Sarah is inventive, then she is responsible.</text>
<logic>responsible(sarah) :- inventive(sarah)</logic>
</step>

The third rule states that being patient forces Sarah to be not calm. This contrapositive relationship will allow us to infer the negation of patient once we have calmness established.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Sarah is patient, then she is not calm.</text>
<logic>~calm(sarah) :- patient(sarah)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction offers two alternatives: either Sarah is not responsible or she is skillful. When we later learn that Sarah is responsible, this clause will compel us to accept the other alternative, namely skillfulness.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Either Sarah is not responsible or she is skillful.</text>
<logic>~responsible(sarah) v skillful(sarah)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Sarah is inventive gives us a concrete starting point. It activates the inventive‑to‑responsible rule, providing the first derived property we need for the chain of reasoning.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Sarah is inventive.</text>
<logic>inventive(sarah)</logic>
</step>

By applying the rule that inventiveness implies responsibility to the fact that Sarah is inventive, we can conclude that Sarah is responsible. This new fact will interact with the disjunction in the next step.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,2">
<text>Since Sarah is inventive and inventive implies responsible, we infer that Sarah is responsible.</text>
<logic>responsible(sarah)</logic>
</step>

Now we have Sarah as responsible, but the disjunction says "either not responsible or skillful". Since the first option is false, the only way for the disjunction to hold is that Sarah must be skillful. Thus we derive skillfulness.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,4">
<text>Given that Sarah is responsible and we know that either she is not responsible or skillful, the only way to avoid contradiction is that she must be skillful.</text>
<logic>skillful(sarah)</logic>
</step>

Having established that Sarah is skillful, we can invoke the rule that skillful implies calm. Consequently, Sarah is calm, giving us a positive calmness fact that will be essential for the final inference about patient.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,1">
<text>Because Sarah is skillful and skillful leads to calm, we conclude Sarah is calm.</text>
<logic>calm(sarah)</logic>
</step>

We now possess the fact that Sarah is calm, and we also have the rule that patient leads to not calm. Since Sarah is calm, the antecedent of that rule cannot be true; therefore Sarah cannot be patient, yielding the negation of patient.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,3">
<text>With Sarah being calm and the rule that patient implies not calm, we deduce that Sarah cannot be patient.</text>
<logic>~patient(sarah)</logic>
</step>

We derived Sarah is not patient, so the conclusion Sarah is patient is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: Farmer_ingrid is watchful on the fields. Tractor_joy is also watchful as it tends the rows. Whenever tractor_joy has soil_rich soil, it becomes bountiful. Either tractor_joy is not watchful, or it has soil_rich soil.
examples.question: Given the information, is tractor_joy bountiful?

<logic>bountiful(tractor_joy) :- soil_rich(tractor_joy)</logic>

The disjunction provides two alternatives concerning tractor_joy: either it lacks watchfulness or it possesses soil_rich soil. This statement will allow us to eliminate one option once we know the tractor's watchfulness status.
<step n="2" type="premise">

<step n="3" type="premise">

</step>

<step n="4" type="premise">

By disjunctive syllogism, the remaining alternative must be true, so tractor_joy indeed has soil_rich soil. This step turns the vague disjunction into a concrete property we can use.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">

Having established soil_rich for tractor_joy, we can now apply the earlier rule that soil_rich implies bountifulness. By existential instantiation we know there exists something (tractor_joy) with soil_rich, and the rule tells us such a thing must be bountiful, giving us the desired property.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>Since tractor_joy has soil_rich soil and the rule linking soil_rich to bountiful, we can conclude that tractor_joy is bountiful.</text>

Therefore Tractor_joy is bountiful, so the conclusion is True.

examples.context: In the economics research department, Clara is known to be intuitive and also logical. Jordan, another analyst, is already recorded as logical. The department follows a set of behavioral guidelines: whenever someone is fair‑minded, they are disciplined; whenever someone is wise, they are energetic; whenever someone is energetic, they are fair‑minded; whenever someone is vigorous, they are intuitive; whenever someone is logical, they are vigorous; and whenever someone is intuitive, they are wise. These rules capture the way personal traits cascade into one another among the economists.
examples.question: Is Jordan disciplined according to the given facts and rules?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether Jordan possesses the disciplined trait. The goal is to chain together the given facts and conditional rules so that we can eventually apply the rule linking fairness to discipline. Starting from the only concrete fact about Jordan—his logical nature—we will see how the rules propagate this property forward.

The first rule gives a direct path from being fair‑minded to being disciplined for Jordan. It will be useful at the end of the chain once we have shown Jordan is fair‑minded. Keeping this implication in mind helps us target the intermediate steps needed to reach fairness.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Jordan is fair‑minded, then Jordan is disciplined.</text>
<logic>disciplined(jordan) :- fair_minded(jordan)</logic>
</step>

The second rule tells us that wisdom leads to energy for Jordan. Since discipline requires fairness, and fairness is tied to energy by another rule, establishing Jordan's wisdom will be a critical intermediate step.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Jordan is wise, then Jordan is energetic.</text>
<logic>energetic(jordan) :- wise(jordan)</logic>
</step>

The third rule creates a loop: energy guarantees fairness. This means that once we can prove Jordan is energetic, we will automatically obtain fairness, which then yields discipline via PREMISE_1.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Jordan is energetic, then Jordan is fair‑minded.</text>
<logic>fair_minded(jordan) :- energetic(jordan)</logic>
</step>

The fourth rule converts vigor into intuition for Jordan. This provides a way to move from a more physical characteristic (vigorous) to a mental one (intuitive), which later connects to wisdom.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Jordan is vigorous, then Jordan is intuitive.</text>
<logic>intuitive(jordan) :- vigorous(jordan)</logic>
</step>

The fifth rule links logic to vigor for Jordan. Because we already know Jordan is logical, this rule will let us infer vigor, setting off the chain that eventually leads to discipline.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Jordan is logical, then Jordan is vigorous.</text>
<logic>vigorous(jordan) :- logical(jordan)</logic>
</step>

The sixth rule says that intuition implies wisdom for Jordan. Once we have intuition, we can immediately claim Jordan is wise, which then activates the energetic rule.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Jordan is intuitive, then Jordan is wise.</text>
<logic>wise(jordan) :- intuitive(jordan)</logic>
</step>

Clara being intuitive is a factual statement, but it does not directly affect Jordan. It is included to complete the list of given facts, though it will not be used in the derivation of Jordan's discipline.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Clara is intuitive.</text>
<logic>intuitive(clara)</logic>
</step>

Clara being logical is another fact that mirrors Jordan's logical status. Like the previous fact, it is not needed for the proof but is part of the supplied information.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>Clara is logical.</text>
<logic>logical(clara)</logic>
</step>

Jordan is logical is the pivotal fact that starts our reasoning chain. Through the logical‑to‑vigorous rule, this fact will allow us to propagate forward toward discipline.
<step n="9" type="premise">
<text>Jordan is logical.</text>
<logic>logical(jordan)</logic>
</step>

Using Jordan's logical status and the rule that logical implies vigorous, we can conclude Jordan is vigorous. This step is an instance of existential instantiation combined with modus ponens: the rule applies directly to the known logical fact.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,5">
<text>From Jordan being logical and the rule that logical implies vigorous, we infer that Jordan is vigorous.</text>
<logic>vigorous(jordan)</logic>
</step>

Having established Jordan's vigor, we invoke the rule that vigor leads to intuition. By modus ponens, Jordan must be intuitive. This moves us from a physical attribute to a mental one, preparing for the next link in the chain.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,4">
<text>Since Jordan is vigorous and vigorous implies intuitive, we conclude Jordan is intuitive.</text>
<logic>intuitive(jordan)</logic>
</step>

From Jordan's intuition and the rule that intuition implies wisdom, we infer Jordan is wise. This step applies universal instantiation of the intuition‑to‑wisdom rule, allowing us to claim wisdom for Jordan specifically.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,6">
<text>Given Jordan is intuitive and the rule that intuitive implies wise, we deduce Jordan is wise.</text>
<logic>wise(jordan)</logic>
</step>

Now that Jordan is wise, we use the rule that wisdom yields energy. Modus ponens gives us Jordan is energetic, positioning us to derive fairness next.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,2">
<text>Because Jordan is wise and wise implies energetic, we obtain that Jordan is energetic.</text>
<logic>energetic(jordan)</logic>
</step>

With Jordan energetic, we apply the rule that energetic individuals are fair‑minded. This gives us Jordan's fairness, which is the exact condition needed to activate the disciplined rule.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,3">
<text>From Jordan being energetic and the rule that energetic implies fair‑minded, we derive that Jordan is fair‑minded.</text>
<logic>fair_minded(jordan)</logic>
</step>

Finally, using the fairness‑to‑discipline rule, we conclude Jordan is disciplined. This completes the chain from the initial logical fact to the desired disciplined property.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,1">
<text>Finally, with Jordan fair‑minded and the rule that fair‑minded implies disciplined, we establish that Jordan is disciplined.</text>
<logic>disciplined(jordan)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Jordan is disciplined, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: In the veterinary records, the animal named fern is noted as analytical. The animal named scout is also recorded as analytical. Both fern and scout are described as proactive. Another rule says that if rain is analytical, then rain is intuitive. No other facts or rules are provided.
examples.question: Is rain not intuitive?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are given a set of facts about three animals and two conditional rules that connect the properties proactive, analytical, and intuitive. Our goal is to determine whether the statement that rain is not intuitive can be justified from the information provided.

This rule tells us how the property proactive for rain leads directly to the property analytical for rain. It will be essential later when we try to turn the known fact that rain is proactive into an analytical statement.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule that if rain is proactive then rain is analytical.</text>
<logic>analytical(rain) :- proactive(rain)</logic>
</step>

The second rule establishes a bridge from analytical to intuitive, but only for rain. Once we have shown that rain is analytical, this rule will allow us to deduce that rain is also intuitive.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule that if rain is analytical then rain is intuitive.</text>
<logic>intuitive(rain) :- analytical(rain)</logic>
</step>

The fact that fern is analytical is part of the background knowledge about the animals. Although it does not involve rain, it illustrates that the analytical predicate is used in the domain.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Fern is analytical.</text>
<logic>analytical(fern)</logic>
</step>

Similarly, scout being analytical adds another example of an animal with that property. These examples help confirm that the analytical predicate is meaningful in the context.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Scout is analytical.</text>
<logic>analytical(scout)</logic>
</step>

Fern being proactive provides a concrete instance of the proactive predicate. It shows that the clinic tracks which animals are proactive, which will be relevant when we apply the rule about proactive rain.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Fern is proactive.</text>
<logic>proactive(fern)</logic>
</step>

Scout is also proactive, giving us another concrete case of the proactive property. Together with the previous proactive facts, it confirms that the proactive predicate is populated in the knowledge base.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Scout is proactive.</text>
<logic>proactive(scout)</logic>
</step>

The crucial fact for our query is that rain is proactive. This directly activates the first rule, allowing us to move from a proactive description of rain to an analytical one.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Rain is proactive.</text>
<logic>proactive(rain)</logic>
</step>

Using the fact that rain is proactive (PREMISE_7) and the conditional rule that proactive rain implies analytical rain (PREMISE_1), we can infer that rain must be analytical. This step is an application of logical implication: the antecedent holds, so the consequent follows.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,1">
<text>From rain being proactive and the rule linking proactive rain to analytical rain, we infer that rain is analytical.</text>
<logic>analytical(rain)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established rain is analytical (STEP_8), we can apply the second rule stating that analytical rain leads to intuitive rain (PREMISE_2). Because the premise of the rule is satisfied, we conclude that rain is intuitive.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,2">
<text>Since rain is now known to be analytical and the rule says analytical rain implies intuitive rain, we conclude that rain is intuitive.</text>
<logic>intuitive(rain)</logic>
</step>

We derived Rain is intuitive, so the conclusion Rain is not intuitive is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: Ari is observant. Whenever something is observant, it is also consistent, so Ari’s observance guarantees her consistency. If anything is cautious, then it is curious; thus cautiousness leads to curiosity. Anything that is organized automatically becomes meticulous, meaning organization implies meticulousness. Conversely, being resourceful forces a lack of consistency, so any resourceful person is not consistent. Moreover, Ari is either resourceful or cautious, and at least one of the two must hold. Finally, either Ari is not curious or she is organized; at least one of those alternatives is true.
examples.question: Based on the given information, can we conclude that Ari is not meticulous?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked whether the premises about Ari allow us to assert that she is not meticulous. To answer, we will follow the chain of implications and disjunctions step by step, checking how each piece of information shapes Ari’s attributes. The goal is to see if we can derive a contradiction to the statement "Ari is not meticulous".

The first rule connects observance to consistency, which will be useful because we already know Ari is observant. By keeping this conditional in mind, we can later turn a fact about observance into a fact about consistency.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Ari is observant, then she is consistent.</text>
<logic>consistent(ari) :- observant(ari)</logic>
</step>

The second rule tells us that cautiousness guarantees curiosity. This will become relevant once we determine whether Ari is cautious, allowing us to move from cautious to curious later in the proof.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that cautiousness implies curiosity, so if Ari were cautious she would be curious.</text>
<logic>curious(ari) :- cautious(ari)</logic>
</step>

The third rule links organization to meticulousness. Since meticulousness is the property we are questioning, establishing Ari’s organization will directly give us a route to infer meticulousness.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule declares that organization guarantees meticulousness; any organized individual is meticulous.</text>
<logic>meticulous(ari) :- organized(ari)</logic>
</step>

The fourth rule provides a way to block resourcefulness: if Ari were resourceful, she would not be consistent. This inverse relationship will help us eliminate the resourceful alternative later on.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The rule asserts that being resourceful makes one not consistent; resourceful Ari would be inconsistent.</text>
<logic>~consistent(ari) :- resourceful(ari)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction states that Ari must be either resourceful or cautious. Knowing that at least one of these holds gives us a fork in the reasoning that we can later resolve using other information.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>We are told that Ari is either resourceful or cautious; one of those must be true.</text>
<logic>resourceful(ari) v cautious(ari)</logic>
</step>

The second disjunction offers another fork: either Ari is not curious or she is organized. This will allow us to force organization once we can rule out the "not curious" option.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>The disjunction says that either Ari is not curious or she is organized; at least one holds.</text>
<logic>~curious(ari) v organized(ari)</logic>
</step>

The factual premise tells us directly that Ari is observant. This concrete fact is the starting point for applying the first rule about consistency.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>It is a fact that Ari is observant.</text>
<logic>observant(ari)</logic>
</step>

Applying Modus Ponens to the fact that Ari is observant and the rule that observance implies consistency yields that Ari is consistent. This step converts a known attribute into a new one that will be needed for the next inference.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,1">
<text>From Ari’s observance and the rule linking observance to consistency, we infer that Ari is consistent.</text>
<logic>consistent(ari)</logic>
</step>

Now we have Ari consistent, but the rule about resourcefulness says a resourceful Ari would be not consistent. Since Ari is in fact consistent, the only way to avoid a contradiction is to conclude that Ari cannot be resourceful. This is a classic use of Modus Tollens, eliminating the resourceful alternative.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,4">
<text>Since Ari is consistent and resourcefulness would make her inconsistent, we can conclude that Ari cannot be resourceful.</text>
<logic>~resourceful(ari)</logic>
</step>

Because we have ruled out resourcefulness and the disjunction insists that Ari is either resourceful or cautious, the cautious alternative must be true. Thus we deduce that Ari is cautious, resolving the first disjunction.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,5">
<text>Given that Ari is not resourceful and she must be either resourceful or cautious, the only remaining option is that Ari is cautious.</text>
<logic>cautious(ari)</logic>
</step>

With Ari now known to be cautious, we can activate the second rule that cautiousness leads to curiosity. Consequently, Ari becomes curious, giving us the positive side of the curiosity variable.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,2">
<text>Because Ari is cautious and cautiousness leads to curiosity, Ari must be curious.</text>
<logic>curious(ari)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction "not curious or organized" now has its left side falsified (Ari is curious), so the only way for the statement to hold is for the right side to be true. Therefore, Ari must be organized. This step uses Disjunctive Syllogism to force organization.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,6">
<text>With Ari being curious, the disjunction that she is either not curious or organized forces the organized side to be true, so Ari is organized.</text>
<logic>organized(ari)</logic>
</step>

Finally, the rule linking organization to meticulousness lets us infer that Ari is meticulous, because we have just established her organization. This completes the chain of reasoning that directly addresses the query about meticulousness.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,3">
<text>Finally, Ari’s organization triggers the rule that organized individuals are meticulous, so Ari is meticulous.</text>
<logic>meticulous(ari)</logic>
</step>

We derived Ari is meticulous, so the conclusion Ari is not meticulous is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: Jules works as a carpenter and is known to be reliable. In this workshop, any carpenter who is reliable is also methodical. Moreover, it is known that Jules is either not methodical or he is careful. These statements together provide the information needed to determine whether Jules is careful.
examples.question: Is Jules a careful carpenter?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide if Jules can be described as careful. To answer, we will use the information about his reliability, the rule linking reliability to methodical behavior, and the given disjunction.

The rule tells us that reliability guarantees methodical work. This means that whenever we know someone is reliable, we can also assert they are methodical.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Jules is reliable, then he is methodical.</text>
<logic>methodical(jules) :- reliable(jules)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction states that Jules is either not methodical or he is careful. It offers two mutually exclusive possibilities, one of which must hold.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>Jules is either not methodical or he is careful.</text>
<logic>~methodical(jules) v careful(jules)</logic>
</step>

We are directly told that Jules is reliable. This fact will allow us to activate the rule from PREMISE_1.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Jules is reliable.</text>
<logic>reliable(jules)</logic>
</step>

Applying modus ponens, we combine Jules's reliability with the rule that reliability implies methodical behavior. Consequently, we can conclude that Jules is methodical.
<step n="4" type="derived" from="3,1">
<text>Since Jules is reliable and reliability implies methodical, we conclude Jules is methodical.</text>
<logic>methodical(jules)</logic>
</step>

Now we have Jules being methodical, which makes the first part of the disjunction (~methodical) false. Since a disjunction is true when at least one component is true, the falsity of the first component forces the second component, that Jules is careful, to be true. Thus we infer that Jules is careful.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>Given that Jules is methodical, the alternative that he is not methodical is false, so the disjunction forces that Jules must be careful.</text>
<logic>careful(jules)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Jules is careful, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: Sofia's latest dish is well-spiced, which means she has a clear-headed approach to seasoning. Her overall flavor profile is balanced, showing that she maintains consistency in taste. Whenever Sofia's dish is well-spiced, it follows a standard recipe. If Sofia follows a standard recipe and there is no exception A, then she is allowed to use the special ingredient. Conversely, if Sofia's flavor profile is balanced and there is no exception B, then she is not allowed to use that ingredient. These statements capture all the facts and conditional relationships that will be used in the reasoning.
examples.question: Is Sofia able to prepare the dish?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide if Sofia can prepare the dish, which corresponds to the predicate can_prepare(sofia). To answer, we need to see whether the premises allow us to infer this property or its negation. The reasoning will follow the chain of facts and rules provided in the context.

I need to determine whether Sofia is can prepare can be derived from the given premises.

The first premise links Sofia's well-spiced dish to the notion of a standard recipe. Recognizing this connection is essential because it lets us derive the normal (standard_recipe) status that appears in later default rules. By noting this implication, we set up the conditions for the permission rules.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Sofia's dish is well-spiced, then it conforms to a standard recipe.</text>
<logic>standard_recipe(sofia) :- well_spiced(sofia)</logic>
</step>

The second premise introduces a default rule that grants permission to use a special ingredient when Sofia follows a standard recipe and no exception A is present. This rule will become relevant once we establish that Sofia indeed follows a standard recipe, which we can obtain from PREMISE_1 together with the fact in PREMISE_4. The absence of exception A is assumed by the default unless contradicted, so we can tentatively consider the permission condition.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The default rule says that if Sofia follows a standard recipe and there is no exception A, then she may use the allowed ingredient.</text>
<logic>allowed_ingredient(sofia) :- standard_recipe(sofia), not exception_a(sofia)</logic>
</step>

The third premise provides the opposite default: if Sofia's flavor is balanced and there is no exception B, then she is not permitted to use the ingredient. This creates a potential conflict with PREMISE_2, but the conflict can only be resolved if we have information about exception B. Since the context gives no evidence about exception B, the rule remains applicable in its default form.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The second default rule asserts that if Sofia's flavor is balanced and there is no exception B, then she may not use the allowed ingredient.</text>
<logic>~allowed_ingredient(sofia) :- balanced_flavor(sofia), not exception_b(sofia)</logic>
</step>

The fourth premise supplies the concrete fact that Sofia's dish is well-spiced. This directly satisfies the antecedent of PREMISE_1, allowing us to infer that Sofia follows a standard recipe. Having this concrete fact anchors the chain of reasoning that leads to the permission defaults.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>It is given that Sofia's dish is well-spiced.</text>
<logic>well_spiced(sofia)</logic>
</step>

The fifth premise tells us that Sofia's flavor profile is balanced. This satisfies the condition of the second default rule (PREMISE_3), meaning that, unless an exception B is known, we can conclude that she is not allowed to use the ingredient. Together with the earlier derived standard recipe, we now have both permission and prohibition conditions in play, but neither connects to the ability to prepare the dish.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>It is also given that Sofia's flavor profile is balanced.</text>
<logic>balanced_flavor(sofia)</logic>
</step>

<step n="6" type="derived">
<text>Searching for rules that conclude Sofia is can prepare: no rule or disjunction has "can_prepare" as a consequent.</text>
<logic>unreachable(can_prepare(sofia))</logic>
</step>

<step n="7" type="derived">
<text>Similarly, no rule concludes Sofia is not can prepare.</text>
<logic>unreachable(~can_prepare(sofia))</logic>
</step>

<step n="8" type="derived">
<text>Neither Sofia is can prepare nor Sofia is not can prepare can be derived from the given premises. The answer is Unknown.</text>
<logic>unknown(can_prepare(sofia))</logic>
</step>

</think>
Answer: Unknown

examples.context: In the workshop, the master carpenter is licensed. The workshop follows a set of default guidelines: Whenever a carpenter is standard and there is no abnormal reason to block certification, the carpenter is considered certified. Whenever a carpenter is licensed and there is no abnormal reason to block senior status, the carpenter is regarded as senior. Whenever a carpenter is senior and there is no abnormal reason to block being standard, the carpenter is deemed standard. No other facts or alternative possibilities are mentioned.
examples.question: Is the master carpenter diligent?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide if the master carpenter possesses the property of being diligent. To answer, we must see which of the given facts and default rules can lead us to that conclusion.

I need to determine whether Master_carpenter is diligent can be derived from the given premises.

This default rule connects the property of being standard to being certified, provided no abnormality blocks eligibility. It will be useful later if we can establish that the master carpenter is standard, because then we could infer certification, although certification itself does not mention diligence.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that any carpenter who is standard and has no abnormal eligibility issue is certified.</text>
<logic>certified(master_carpenter) :- standard(master_carpenter), not ab_certified(master_carpenter)</logic>
</step>

Here we have a rule that turns a licensed carpenter into a senior one, assuming no abnormality interferes with senior status. Since we already know the master carpenter is licensed, this rule gives us a way to derive seniority, which is a stepping stone toward establishing standardness.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that any carpenter who is licensed and has no abnormal seniority issue is senior.</text>
<logic>senior(master_carpenter) :- licensed(master_carpenter), not ab_senior(master_carpenter)</logic>
</step>

This rule tells us that a senior carpenter becomes standard when there is no abnormality concerning the standard property. If we can obtain seniority from the previous rule, we can then infer that the master carpenter is standard, which will allow the first rule to fire.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that any carpenter who is senior and has no abnormal standard issue is standard.</text>
<logic>standard(master_carpenter) :- senior(master_carpenter), not ab_standard(master_carpenter)</logic>
</step>

The fact that the master carpenter is licensed is the only explicit fact we start with. It activates the second default rule, allowing us to infer seniority, and subsequently standardness and certification, assuming the relevant abnormality conditions are absent.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The master carpenter is licensed.</text>
<logic>licensed(master_carpenter)</logic>
</step>

<step n="5" type="derived">
<text>Searching for rules that conclude Master_carpenter is diligent: no rule or disjunction has "diligent" as a consequent.</text>
<logic>unreachable(diligent(master_carpenter))</logic>
</step>

<step n="6" type="derived">
<text>Similarly, no rule concludes Master_carpenter is not diligent.</text>
<logic>unreachable(~diligent(master_carpenter))</logic>
</step>

<step n="7" type="derived">
<text>Neither Master_carpenter is diligent nor Master_carpenter is not diligent can be derived from the given premises. The answer is Unknown.</text>
<logic>unknown(diligent(master_carpenter))</logic>
</step>

</think>
Answer: Unknown

examples.context: In the kingdom of Eldoria, the orc warrior is versatile. The elf archer is supportive and accurate. The dwarf miner is accurate. Whenever a character is accurate, they are observant. If a character is observant, then they are supportive. If a character is supportive, then they are determined. If a character is upbeat, then they are careful. Being versatile guarantees that the character is hopeful. Moreover, the orc warrior is either not hopeful or is upbeat.
examples.question: Is the dwarf miner not determined?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether the dwarf miner fails to be determined. To answer, we must see if the knowledge base forces the dwarf miner to be determined, which would make the negation false.

The premise that accuracy entails observance is important because it lets us turn a factual claim about precision into a claim about attentiveness. This connection will be used later to propagate properties from the dwarf miner.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule that any character who is accurate is also observant.</text>
<logic>observant(dwarf_miner) :- accurate(dwarf_miner)</logic>
</step>

The premise linking support to determination tells us that once we know an entity is supportive, we can immediately infer it is determined. This will be the final bridge to the query about being not determined.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule that any character who is supportive becomes determined.</text>
<logic>determined(dwarf_miner) :- supportive(dwarf_miner)</logic>
</step>

The premise that observance leads to support creates a chain: accurate → observant → supportive. Recognizing this chain is essential for moving from the dwarf miner's precise status to a supportive one.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule that any character who is observant is supportive.</text>
<logic>supportive(dwarf_miner) :- observant(dwarf_miner)</logic>
</step>

Stating that the orc warrior is versatile gives us a fact about a different character; although not directly used in the query, it completes the set of given facts and participates in the disjunction about hopefulness.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The fact that the orc warrior is versatile.</text>
<logic>versatile(orc_warrior)</logic>
</step>

The fact that the elf archer is supportive provides an example of the supportive predicate in the world, reinforcing that the rule from support to determination is applicable to multiple characters.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The fact that the elf archer is supportive.</text>
<logic>supportive(elf_archer)</logic>
</step>

The elf archer's accuracy is another concrete fact that, together with the accuracy‑to‑observant rule, shows how the system propagates properties from precision to higher‑level traits.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>The fact that the elf archer is accurate.</text>
<logic>accurate(elf_archer)</logic>
</step>

The dwarf miner being accurate is the critical factual starting point for our chain of reasoning about the dwarf miner's status.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>The fact that the dwarf miner is accurate.</text>
<logic>accurate(dwarf_miner)</logic>
</step>

Using the accuracy‑to‑observant rule (PREMISE_1) together with the dwarf miner's accuracy (PREMISE_7), we can conclude the dwarf miner is observant. This step establishes the first link in the chain leading toward determination.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,1">
<text>From the dwarf miner's accuracy and the accuracy‑to‑observant rule, we infer that the dwarf miner is observant.</text>
<logic>observant(dwarf_miner)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have the dwarf miner as observant, we apply the observant‑to‑supportive rule (PREMISE_3) to infer that the dwarf miner is supportive. This moves us one step closer to the property we need for the final claim.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,3">
<text>Since the dwarf miner is observant and observance implies support, we conclude the dwarf miner is supportive.</text>
<logic>supportive(dwarf_miner)</logic>
</step>

With the dwarf miner confirmed as supportive, the supportive‑to‑determined rule (PREMISE_2) allows us to deduce that the dwarf miner is determined. At this point we have directly opposite evidence to the query's negation.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,2">
<text>Given the dwarf miner is supportive and support leads to determination, we deduce the dwarf miner is determined.</text>
<logic>determined(dwarf_miner)</logic>
</step>

We derived Dwarf_miner is determined, so the conclusion Dwarf_miner is not determined is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: Paul is clear headed, and Hayden is enthusiastic while Kate enjoys good health. Whenever someone is fit, that person is also active, so fit(paul) would make Paul active. If Kate is curious, then she is cooperative; likewise, any healthy individual is diligent, which makes Kate diligent because she is healthy. Being a patient guarantees empathy, so any patient is empathetic, and any enthusiastic person is trustworthy, meaning Hayden would be trustworthy because he is enthusiastic. Wisdom follows from clear headedness, so Paul is wise because he is clear headed. A lack of trustworthiness follows from being well prepared, therefore if someone is well prepared they are not trustworthy. Finally, there are three exclusive alternatives: either Hayden is well prepared or Hayden is a patient; either Kate is not diligent or Kate is curious; and either Paul is not wise or Paul is fit.
examples.question: Is Hayden empathetic?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether Hayden possesses the empathetic quality. The premises give us facts about Hayden's enthusiasm and several conditional relationships that connect enthusiasm, trustworthiness, well‑preparedness, and empathy, so we will follow those links step by step.

This premise provides the crucial bridge from being a patient to being empathetic. It tells us that once we can establish Hayden as a patient, empathy will follow automatically.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if someone is a patient then they are empathetic.</text>
<logic>empathetic(hayden) :- patient(hayden)</logic>
</step>

Here we learn that enthusiasm guarantees trustworthiness. Because we already know Hayden is enthusiastic, this rule will later let us infer his trustworthiness.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that any enthusiastic person is trustworthy.</text>
<logic>trustworthy(hayden) :- enthusiastic(hayden)</logic>
</step>

This rule gives us the opposite direction: if someone is well prepared, they cannot be trustworthy. It will be useful for eliminating the well‑prepared alternative later on.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule indicates that if someone is well prepared then they are not trustworthy.</text>
<logic>~trustworthy(hayden) :- well_prepared(hayden)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction supplies two possible states for Hayden: either he is well prepared or he is a patient. One of these must hold, and our goal is to pinpoint which one.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Either Hayden is well prepared or Hayden is a patient.</text>
<logic>well_prepared(hayden) v patient(hayden)</logic>
</step>

Paul being clear headed is recorded, though it does not directly affect Hayden; it simply satisfies a separate rule about wisdom.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Paul is clear headed.</text>
<logic>clear_headed(paul)</logic>
</step>

Kate's health is noted, which will later support her diligence via another rule, but it is not needed for the current query.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Kate is healthy.</text>
<logic>healthy(kate)</logic>
</step>

Hayden's enthusiasm is a fact that activates the rule linking enthusiasm to trustworthiness, setting up the first inference chain.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Hayden is enthusiastic.</text>
<logic>enthusiastic(hayden)</logic>
</step>

Using Hayden's known enthusiasm together with the rule that enthusiastic individuals are trustworthy, we can confidently assert that Hayden is trustworthy. This step applies a straightforward conditional inference.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,2">
<text>From Hayden's enthusiasm and the rule linking enthusiasm to trustworthiness, we infer that Hayden is trustworthy.</text>
<logic>trustworthy(hayden)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established Hayden's trustworthiness, we turn to the rule that well‑preparedness leads to a lack of trustworthiness. Since Hayden is trustworthy, the antecedent of that rule must be false; therefore, Hayden cannot be well prepared. This is a classic contrapositive move.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,3">
<text>Because Hayden is trustworthy and the rule that well prepared implies not trustworthy, we conclude that Hayden cannot be well prepared.</text>
<logic>~well_prepared(hayden)</logic>
</step>

We know from the disjunction that Hayden must be either well prepared or a patient. Because we have just shown he cannot be well prepared, the only remaining option is that he is a patient. This elimination uses disjunctive reasoning.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,4">
<text>Given that Hayden is not well prepared and the disjunction that he is either well prepared or a patient, we deduce that Hayden must be a patient.</text>
<logic>patient(hayden)</logic>
</step>

Having determined that Hayden is a patient, we can finally apply the rule that every patient is empathetic. This gives us the desired conclusion that Hayden is empathetic.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,1">
<text>Since Hayden is a patient and the rule that patients are empathetic, we derive that Hayden is empathetic.</text>
<logic>empathetic(hayden)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Hayden is empathetic, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: Captain Ace is an adaptable pilot. Whenever a pilot has high energy, they are a clear communicator. If a pilot is team supportive, then they are crew caring. Being crew caring guarantees high energy. Any adaptable pilot is also team supportive.
examples.question: Is it true that Captain Ace is not a clear communicator?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to decide whether the statement that Captain Ace is not a clear communicator holds. The goal is to see if the premises force Captain Ace to be a clear communicator, which would make the negation impossible.

The first rule tells us that high energy implies clear communication. This will be useful later when we can establish that Captain Ace has high energy.

Similarly, Pearl being systematic is a factual detail that fulfills the general claim about systematic individuals without influencing the specific derivation for Dawn.

The crucial fact for our reasoning is that Dawn herself is systematic. This concrete piece of information activates the first conditional rule linking systematic to ambitious.

examples.context: In the reserve, the elephant is known to be courageous. The tiger, on the other hand, is wise. Whenever something is courageous, it is also swift, so the elephant being courageous guarantees that it is swift. Any wise creature becomes watchful, which means the tiger being wise makes it watchful. If something is skillful, it is compassionate; therefore a skillful tiger would be compassionate. Being resourceful leads to being initiating, so any resourceful elephant is initiating. Not being swift follows from being migration focused, i.e., if the elephant is migration focused then it cannot be swift. The elephant is either migration focused or resourceful, presenting a clear alternative. Lastly, the tiger is either not watchful or skillful, giving another disjunctive option.
examples.question: Is the elephant initiating?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether the elephant possesses the initiating trait. To answer this, we will trace the chain of properties given for the elephant and apply the relevant conditional statements.

The first rule connects courage to swiftness; any creature that is courageous is automatically swift. This will be useful once we establish the elephant's courage, which we already have as a fact.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that any courageous elephant is also swift.</text>
<logic>swift(elephant) :- courageous(elephant)</logic>
</step>

The second rule tells us that resourcefulness guarantees initiating behavior. If we can show the elephant is resourceful, we will directly obtain the desired conclusion.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that any resourceful elephant is also initiating.</text>
<logic>initiating(elephant) :- resourceful(elephant)</logic>
</step>

The third rule provides a negative relationship: being migration focused prevents an animal from being swift. This contrapositive insight will allow us to eliminate the migration‑focused option later.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if the elephant is migration focused, then it is not swift.</text>
<logic>~swift(elephant) :- migration_focused(elephant)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction offers two possibilities for the elephant: it is either migration focused or resourceful. One of these must hold, but we do not yet know which.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The disjunction tells us that the elephant is either migration focused or resourceful.</text>
<logic>migration_focused(elephant) v resourceful(elephant)</logic>
</step>

The fact that the tiger is wise is recorded for completeness, though it does not directly influence the elephant's initiating status in the subsequent steps.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>It is given that the tiger is wise.</text>
<logic>wise(tiger)</logic>
</step>

The fact that the elephant is courageous is crucial because it activates the rule linking courage to swiftness, giving us a concrete starting point.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>It is given that the elephant is courageous.</text>
<logic>courageous(elephant)</logic>
</step>

Given the elephant's courage and the rule that courage implies swiftness, we can confidently assert that the elephant is swift. This establishes a positive attribute that will later interact with the negative rule about migration focus.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,1">
<text>From the elephant's courage and the rule linking courage to swiftness, we infer that the elephant is swift.</text>
<logic>swift(elephant)</logic>
</step>

We now have the elephant being swift, and the rule that migration focus would make it not swift. By contraposition (modus tollens), the elephant cannot be migration focused; otherwise it would contradict its swiftness. Hence we derive that the elephant is not migration focused.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,3">
<text>Since the elephant is swift and we know that migration focus would prevent swiftness, we conclude that the elephant cannot be migration focused.</text>
<logic>~migration_focused(elephant)</logic>
</step>

Recall the disjunction stating the elephant is either migration focused or resourceful. Since we have just shown it is not migration focused, the only remaining viable alternative is that the elephant is resourceful. This resolves the disjunction in favor of resourcefulness.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,4">
<text>With the knowledge that the elephant is not migration focused and the earlier alternative that it is either migration focused or resourceful, we deduce that the elephant must be resourceful.</text>
<logic>resourceful(elephant)</logic>
</step>

With the elephant established as resourceful, we invoke the rule that resourcefulness leads to initiating. Therefore, the elephant must be initiating, achieving the goal of the query.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,2">
<text>Because the elephant is resourceful and resourcefulness implies initiating, we infer that the elephant is initiating.</text>
<logic>initiating(elephant)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Elephant is initiating, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True
