The disjunction gives us a relationship between not being well organized and being checked out. It will help us move from knowing the novel is well organized to concluding it is checked out.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Either the novel is not well organized, or it is checked out.</text>
<logic>~well_organized(novel) v checked_out(novel)</logic>
</step>

The fact that the reference book is informative is not directly involved in the query, but it mirrors the pattern of informativeness in the domain and confirms the rule's applicability.
<step n="4" type="premise">

</step>

The novel being informative is the crucial fact that activates the first rule, allowing us to infer further properties about the novel.

<logic>informative(novel)</logic>
</step>

Since we have established that the novel is informative (Premise 5) and the rule says any informative novel is well organized (Premise 1), we can directly conclude the novel is well organized. This step bridges the factual information to the conditional property.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">

<logic>well_organized(novel)</logic>
</step>

We now know the novel is well organized (Step 6). The disjunction (Premise 3) states that either the novel is not well organized or it is checked out. Because the first alternative is false, the only way the disjunction holds is if the novel is checked out. Hence we infer the checked‑out status.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">

Having shown the novel is checked out (Step 7) and using the rule that any checked‑out novel is quiet (Premise 2), we can immediately infer that the novel is quiet. This completes the chain of reasoning needed for the query.

examples.context: In the wildlife conservation team, Helen is known to be capable. The team notes that whenever Helen is not attentive, she becomes astute. examples.question: Based on the given information, can we conclude that Helen is not motivated?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether Helen lacks motivation according to the supplied statements. The goal is to derive the negation of motivated(helen) from the facts and rules provided.

The first rule tells us that a lack of attentiveness guarantees astuteness. This conditional will become useful once we have established that Helen is not attentive, allowing us to produce the property astute.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Helen is not attentive, then she is astute.</text>
<logic>astute(helen) :- ~attentive(helen)</logic>
</step>

The second rule expresses that motivation forces the opposite of astuteness. By later showing Helen is astute, we will be able to turn this rule around and conclude that she cannot be motivated.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Helen is motivated, then she is not astute.</text>
<logic>~astute(helen) :- motivated(helen)</logic>
</step>

The third rule links attentiveness to the absence of dedication. Knowing whether Helen is dedicated will let us work backwards to learn about her attentiveness, which is needed for the first rule.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Helen is attentive, then she is not dedicated.</text>
<logic>~dedicated(helen) :- attentive(helen)</logic>
</step>

The first disjunction offers two alternatives: Helen is either not capable or she is meticulous. Because we already know she is capable, the only way for the statement to hold is that she must be meticulous.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Either Helen is not capable or she is meticulous.</text>
<logic>~capable(helen) v meticulous(helen)</logic>
</step>

The second disjunction says Helen is either not meticulous or dedicated. Once we have shown she is meticulous, the only remaining way for the disjunction to be true is that she must be dedicated.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Either Helen is not meticulous or she is dedicated.</text>
<logic>~meticulous(helen) v dedicated(helen)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Helen is capable is the starting point for the chain of deductions. It directly interacts with the first disjunction to let us infer a new property.

<text>Helen is capable.</text>
<logic>capable(helen)</logic>
</step>

Having both the fact capable(helen) and the disjunction ~capable(helen) ∨ meticulous(helen), we apply disjunctive syllogism: the left side of the disjunction is false, so the right side must hold. Therefore, Helen is meticulous.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,4">
<text>From Helen being capable and the first disjunction we infer that Helen is meticulous.</text>
<logic>meticulous(helen)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know meticulous(helen) and have the disjunction ~meticulous(helen) ∨ dedicated(helen), we again use disjunctive syllogism. The left option is false, forcing the right option to be true, so Helen is dedicated.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,5">
<text>Using the fact that Helen is meticulous and the second disjunction we infer that Helen is dedicated.</text>
<logic>dedicated(helen)</logic>
</step>

From dedicated(helen) and the rule ~dedicated(helen) :- attentive(helen) (which reads attentive → not dedicated), we use the contrapositive: if someone is dedicated, they cannot be attentive. Thus we conclude that Helen is not attentive.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,3">
<text>Since Helen is dedicated and the rule linking attentiveness to non‑dedication, we conclude that Helen is not attentive.</text>
<logic>~attentive(helen)</logic>

With the established fact ~attentive(helen) and the rule astute(helen) :- ~attentive(helen), modus ponens gives us astute(helen). This new property will be crucial for the final step.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,1">
<text>Given that Helen is not attentive, the first rule lets us deduce that Helen is astute.</text>
<logic>astute(helen)</logic>
</step>

We have astute(helen) and the rule ~astute(helen) :- motivated(helen) (motivated → not astute). Taking the contrapositive, if someone is astute, they cannot be motivated. Hence we derive ~motivated(helen).
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,2">
<text>Because Helen is astute and the second rule ties motivation to non‑astuteness, we infer that Helen is not motivated.</text>
<logic>~motivated(helen)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Helen is not motivated, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

Being observant follows from being efficient, so any efficient entity is observant. An inventive entity guarantees systematic behavior, meaning any inventive thing is systematic. Trustworthiness leads to the negation of articulateness: if someone is trustworthy, then they are not articulate. Finally, Kent is either trustworthy or evidence‑driven; at least one of those two properties holds for him.
examples.question: Is it false that Kent is not analytical?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether the claim that Kent is not analytical holds. To answer, we must trace the chain of properties given in the context and see if analytical(kent) can be derived. If analytical(kent) follows, then the negation is false.

The first rule tells us that systematic status guarantees analyticity. This will become important later when we manage to show Kent is systematic, because it will directly give us the analytical property we need for the query.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>Any systematic Kent is also analytical.</text>
<logic>analytical(kent) :- systematic(kent)</logic>

The second rule connects competitiveness to articulateness. Knowing whether Kent is competitive will let us infer his articulateness, which later interacts with the trustworthiness rule.

<text>If Kent is competitive, then he is articulate.</text>
<logic>articulate(kent) :- competitive(kent)</logic>
</step>

The third rule says that being observant forces competitiveness. This creates a bridge from an earlier fact (efficiency → observant) toward competitiveness, and ultimately toward articulateness.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Being observant guarantees that Kent is competitive.</text>
<logic>competitive(kent) :- observant(kent)</logic>
</step>

The fourth rule explains that evidence‑driven status yields inventiveness. Since we will later obtain evidence‑driven(kent) from the disjunction, this rule will let us move toward systematic behavior.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>When Kent is evidence‑driven, he becomes inventive.</text>
<logic>inventive(kent) :- evidence_driven(kent)</logic>
</step>

The fifth rule directly links efficiency to observance. Because we already know Kent is efficient, this rule immediately supplies us with observant(kent), kicking off the chain of deductions.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Efficiency implies observance, so Kent’s efficiency makes him observant.</text>
<logic>observant(kent) :- efficient(kent)</logic>
</step>

The sixth rule makes inventive entities systematic. Once we have inventiveness, this rule will give us systematic(kent), which is the key to establishing analyticity.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Inventiveness forces systematic behavior, linking Kent’s inventiveness to being systematic.</text>
<logic>systematic(kent) :- inventive(kent)</logic>
</step>

The seventh rule is a negative implication: trustworthiness would forbid articulateness. This will allow us to use a contradiction later to eliminate trustworthiness and force the alternative in the disjunction.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>If Kent were trustworthy, then he could not be articulate.</text>
<logic>~articulate(kent) :- trustworthy(kent)</logic>
</step>

The eighth premise is a disjunction stating that Kent must be either trustworthy or evidence‑driven. By eliminating the trustworthy option, we will be compelled to accept evidence‑driven(kent).
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>Either Kent is trustworthy or Kent is evidence‑driven.</text>
<logic>trustworthy(kent) v evidence_driven(kent)</logic>
</step>

The ninth premise is the concrete fact that Kent is efficient. This is the starting point for the forward reasoning that will eventually lead to analytical(kent).
<step n="9" type="premise">
<text>Kent is efficient.</text>
<logic>efficient(kent)</logic>
</step>

Using the fact that Kent is efficient and the rule that efficiency implies observance, we can confidently assert that Kent is observant. This step is a direct application of modus ponens with premise 5.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,5">
<text>From Kent’s efficiency and the rule that efficiency leads to observance, we infer that Kent is observant.</text>
<logic>observant(kent)</logic>
</step>

Having established that Kent is observant, we apply the rule that observant entities are competitive. This yields competitive(kent), again by modus ponens, advancing us toward articulateness.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,3">
<text>Since observant Kent is competitive by the relevant rule, we deduce that Kent is competitive.</text>
<logic>competitive(kent)</logic>
</step>

From competitive(kent) and the rule linking competitiveness to articulateness, we infer that Kent is articulate. This derivation is straightforward modus ponens using premise 2.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,2">
<text>From Kent’s competitiveness and the rule linking competitiveness to articulateness, we obtain that Kent is articulate.</text>
<logic>articulate(kent)</logic>
</step>

We now have articulate(kent). The rule saying trustworthy(kent) leads to ~articulate(kent) allows us to use modus tollens: if Kent were trustworthy, he could not be articulate, but we see he is articulate, so trustworthy(kent) must be false. Hence ~trustworthy(kent).
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,7">
<text>Because Kent is articulate and trustworthiness would make him not articulate, we conclude that Kent cannot be trustworthy.</text>
<logic>~trustworthy(kent)</logic>
</step>

With ~trustworthy(kent) and the disjunction that Kent is either trustworthy or evidence‑driven, the only way to satisfy the disjunction is for Kent to be evidence‑driven. This is a classic disjunctive syllogism, giving us evidence_driven(kent).
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,8">
<text>Given that Kent is not trustworthy and the disjunction that he is either trustworthy or evidence‑driven, we must accept that Kent is evidence‑driven.</text>

Now that Kent is evidence‑driven, the rule that evidence‑driven implies inventive lets us conclude inventive(kent). This is another modus ponens step, moving us closer to systematic status.

We derived Kent is analytical, so the conclusion Kent is not analytical is False.

The disjunction presents two alternatives: either Detective Jones is not accurate, or he is a sharp observer. Once we settle the truth of one side, we can infer the other.

<text>Detective Jones is a quick thinker.</text>

examples.context: Derek is known to be evidence-driven. Whenever Derek is patient, he is also meticulous. If Derek happens to be energetic, then he is not meticulous. In addition, either Derek is not evidence-driven or Derek is patient. No other default assumptions are made.
examples.question: Can we conclude that Derek is energetic?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are given a mixture of facts, conditional rules, and a disjunction about Derek. Our goal is to see whether the statement "Derek is energetic" can be established from this information. To do that we will follow the chain of implications step by step.

This premise links patientness to meticulousness, which will be useful later when we manage to prove that Derek is patient. It tells us that patient status guarantees meticulous status.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Derek is patient then Derek is meticulous.</text>
<logic>meticulous(derek) :- patient(derek)</logic>
</step>

This premise connects energetic behavior to the lack of meticulousness. It will allow us to reason about energeticness by examining whether Derek is meticulous, using a contrapositive argument.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Derek is energetic then Derek is not meticulous.</text>
<logic>~meticulous(derek) :- energetic(derek)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction gives us two possibilities: either Derek is not evidence-driven or he is patient. Combined with the fact that Derek is evidence-driven, it forces the second alternative to hold, giving us a route to patientness.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The statement provides a disjunction: either Derek is not evidence-driven or Derek is patient.</text>
<logic>~evidence_driven(derek) v patient(derek)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Derek is evidence-driven is essential because it interacts with the disjunction to eliminate the "not evidence-driven" option, paving the way for the derivation of patientness.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>It is a fact that Derek is evidence-driven.</text>
<logic>evidence_driven(derek)</logic>
</step>

Since we know Derek is evidence-driven, the disjunct "not evidence-driven" cannot be true. In a disjunction, if one option is false, the other must be true, so we conclude Derek is patient. This is a classic use of disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,3">
<text>Because Derek is evidence-driven and the disjunction says either not evidence-driven or patient, we can eliminate the first option and infer that Derek must be patient.</text>
<logic>patient(derek)</logic>
</step>

Having established that Derek is patient, we can now apply the conditional from PREMISE_1. The rule says patient implies meticulous, so by modus ponens we infer that Derek is meticulous. This gives us a concrete property to work with in the next step.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>From Derek being patient and the rule that patient implies meticulous, we infer that Derek is meticulous.</text>
<logic>meticulous(derek)</logic>
</step>

We now have Derek meticulous, but PREMISE_2 tells us that energetic would lead to not meticulous. Taking the contrapositive (modus tollens), if Derek were energetic, he would not be meticulous, yet we have the opposite. Therefore Derek cannot be energetic, and we derive the negation of the query.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">
<text>Since we now have Derek meticulous and the rule that energetic implies not meticulous, the contrapositive tells us Derek cannot be energetic, so we derive that Derek is not energetic.</text>
<logic>~energetic(derek)</logic>
</step>

We derived Derek is not energetic, so the conclusion Derek is energetic is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: At the downtown library four staff members—cameron, kendall, olga, and scout—are noted for their professional habits. Cameron, olga, and scout are all witty. Cameron, kendall, olga, and scout are each proficient. The library’s guidelines state that any proficient librarian is efficient. Whenever a librarian is witty, that librarian is punctual. Whenever a librarian is punctual, that librarian is consistent. If a librarian is cooperative, then that librarian is not efficient. For kendall specifically, either kendall is cooperative or kendall is witty. No other default rules apply.
examples.question: Is it true that kendall is not consistent?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to decide whether the statement that kendall is not consistent holds. The goal is to see if the premises force kendall to be consistent, which would make the negation false.

This premise links punctuality to consistency, so any later proof that kendall is punctual will immediately give us consistency. It sets up the final step of the argument.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that any punctual librarian is also consistent.</text>
<logic>consistent(kendall) :- punctual(kendall)</logic>
</step>

Here we are told that proficiency guarantees efficiency. Since we already have a fact that kendall is proficient, this rule will let us derive that kendall is efficient, a crucial intermediate fact.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that any proficient librarian is efficient.</text>
<logic>efficient(kendall) :- proficient(kendall)</logic>
</step>

This rule connects wit to punctuality. Later we will aim to establish that kendall is witty, and then we can use this premise to get punctuality, moving toward consistency.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that any witty librarian is punctual.</text>
<logic>punctual(kendall) :- witty(kendall)</logic>
</step>

The rule states that if someone is cooperative they cannot be efficient. This will allow us to argue that kendall is not cooperative once we have shown kendall is efficient.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that any cooperative librarian is not efficient.</text>
<logic>~efficient(kendall) :- cooperative(kendall)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction gives us two possibilities for kendall: cooperative or witty. By eliminating one alternative we can force the other, which will be essential for reaching punctuality.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>For kendall, either kendall is cooperative or kendall is witty.</text>
<logic>cooperative(kendall) v witty(kendall)</logic>
</step>

<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>cameron is proficient.</text>
<logic>proficient(cameron)</logic>
</step>

<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>olga is proficient.</text>
<logic>proficient(olga)</logic>
</step>

<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>scout is proficient.</text>
<logic>proficient(scout)</logic>
</step>

<step n="9" type="premise">
<text>cameron is witty.</text>
<logic>witty(cameron)</logic>
</step>

<step n="10" type="premise">
<text>olga is witty.</text>
<logic>witty(olga)</logic>
</step>

<step n="11" type="premise">
<text>scout is witty.</text>
<logic>witty(scout)</logic>
</step>

<step n="12" type="premise">
<text>kendall is proficient.</text>
<logic>proficient(kendall)</logic>
</step>

Using the fact that kendall is proficient together with the rule that proficiency implies efficiency, we conclude kendall is efficient. This establishes the premise needed for the next contradiction step.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,2">
<text>Because kendall is proficient and proficiency guarantees efficiency, we infer that kendall is efficient.</text>
<logic>efficient(kendall)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know kendall is efficient, the rule that cooperation leads to not‑efficient creates a conflict if kendall were cooperative. To avoid inconsistency, we infer that kendall cannot be cooperative, i.e., ~cooperative(kendall).
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,4">
<text>Since kendall is efficient and cooperation would make a librarian not efficient, we conclude that kendall cannot be cooperative.</text>
<logic>~cooperative(kendall)</logic>
</step>

We have the disjunction that kendall is either cooperative or witty, and we have just shown kendall is not cooperative. The only way the disjunction can remain true is if kendall is witty, so we derive witty(kendall).
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,5">
<text>Given that kendall is not cooperative and the disjunction that kendall is either cooperative or witty, we deduce that kendall must be witty.</text>
<logic>witty(kendall)</logic>
</step>

Having established that kendall is witty, we apply the rule that wit guarantees punctuality. This yields punctual(kendall), moving us one step closer to consistency.
<step n="16" type="derived" from="15,3">
<text>Because kendall is witty and wit leads to punctuality, we infer that kendall is punctual.</text>
<logic>punctual(kendall)</logic>
</step>

Finally, with punctual(kendall) in hand, the first rule tells us that any punctual librarian is consistent. Therefore we infer consistent(kendall).
<step n="17" type="derived" from="16,1">
<text>With kendall punctual and the rule that punctuality leads to consistency, we derive that kendall is consistent.</text>
<logic>consistent(kendall)</logic>
</step>

We derived Kendall is consistent, so the conclusion Kendall is not consistent is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: In this chess strategy setting, the position called king_castle is aggressive. Whenever a position is under_threat, it is seasoned. examples.question: Is the king_castle seasoned?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether the king_castle position is seasoned. To answer, we need to use the given facts and the rule that connects being under_threat to being seasoned.

The rule that under_threat implies seasoned is central because it provides the bridge from a threat condition to the seasoned property we are trying to prove. Recognizing this conditional will allow us to derive seasoned once we establish under_threat for king_castle.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that any position that is under_threat is seasoned.</text>
<logic>seasoned(king_castle) :- under_threat(king_castle)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction tells us that at least one of two possibilities holds: either king_castle is not aggressive or it is under_threat. This information will be useful together with the fact about aggressiveness to eliminate the first alternative and obtain under_threat.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The statement says the king_castle is either not aggressive or under_threat.</text>
<logic>~aggressive(king_castle) v under_threat(king_castle)</logic>
</step>

We are explicitly told that king_castle is aggressive. This fact directly contradicts the "not aggressive" part of the disjunction, forcing the other side of the disjunction to be true.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>It is given that the king_castle is aggressive.</text>
<logic>aggressive(king_castle)</logic>
</step>

Because king_castle is aggressive, the "not aggressive" option in the disjunction cannot hold. Therefore, the only way the disjunction remains true is that king_castle is under_threat. This uses the principle of disjunctive syllogism to isolate the under_threat condition.
<step n="4" type="derived" from="3,2">
<text>From the fact that the king_castle is aggressive and the disjunction, we infer that the king_castle must be under_threat.</text>
<logic>under_threat(king_castle)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established king_castle is under_threat, we can apply the rule from PREMISE_1. The rule states that any under_threat position is seasoned, so we can directly conclude that king_castle is seasoned. This is a straightforward modus ponens step.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,1">
<text>Since the king_castle is under_threat and the rule links under_threat to seasoned, we conclude the king_castle is seasoned.</text>
<logic>seasoned(king_castle)</logic>
</step>

Therefore King_castle is seasoned, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

The fact that lead_climber is experienced is crucial because, together with rule PREMISE_2, it allows us to infer vigor, and then, via PREMISE_1, carefulness. However, carefulness still does not connect to thoroughness.

Rope_master being experienced adds another instance of the experienced predicate, reinforcing the rule chain for other climbers, but it does not give new information about the lead climber.

examples.context: Sasha is reflective. Whenever Sasha is well prepared, Sasha becomes focused. Being impartial guarantees that Sasha is patient. If Sasha is proficient, then Sasha is not patient. Sasha is either proficient or judicious. Either Sasha is not focused or Sasha is impartial. Either Sasha is not reflective or Sasha is well prepared.
examples.question: Is Sasha judicious?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked whether Sasha possesses the quality of being judicious. To answer this, we will chain together the given facts, rules, and disjunctions until we can derive the judicious predicate for Sasha. The proof proceeds by eliminating alternatives and applying the conditional statements step by step.

The first rule connects well‑preparedness to focus, which will become useful once we know Sasha is well prepared. It tells us that establishing well preparation is enough to obtain focus later in the chain.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Sasha is well prepared, then Sasha is focused.</text>
<logic>focused(sasha) :- well_prepared(sasha)</logic>
</step>

The second rule states that impartiality guarantees patience. This will allow us to move from impartial to patient once we have derived impartiality from another premise.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Sasha is impartial, then Sasha is patient.</text>
<logic>patient(sasha) :- impartial(sasha)</logic>
</step>

The third rule gives a contrapositive‑style relationship: if Sasha were proficient, she could not be patient. This will help us later to rule out proficiency once we have shown Sasha is patient.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Sasha is proficient, then Sasha is not patient.</text>
<logic>~patient(sasha) :- proficient(sasha)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction that Sasha is either proficient or judicious sets up a binary choice. By later eliminating the proficient alternative, we will be forced to accept the judicious one.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Sasha is either proficient or judicious.</text>
<logic>proficient(sasha) v judicious(sasha)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction about focus and impartiality ensures that if Sasha is not focused, she must be impartial. When we later learn Sasha is focused, this clause will let us deduce impartiality directly.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Either Sasha is not focused or Sasha is impartial.</text>
<logic>~focused(sasha) v impartial(sasha)</logic>
</step>

The last disjunction links reflectiveness to well preparation, asserting that either Sasha is not reflective or she is well prepared. Combined with the fact that Sasha is reflective, this will let us infer well preparation.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Either Sasha is not reflective or Sasha is well prepared.</text>
<logic>~reflective(sasha) v well_prepared(sasha)</logic>
</step>

We are given outright that Sasha is reflective. This fact will interact with the previous disjunction to produce a concrete conclusion about her preparation status.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Sasha is reflective.</text>
<logic>reflective(sasha)</logic>
</step>

Because Sasha is reflective, the clause "either not reflective or well prepared" cannot be satisfied by the first option, leaving well preparation as the only viable alternative. Thus we can confidently conclude Sasha is well prepared.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,6">
<text>From Sasha being reflective and the statement that either she is not reflective or she is well prepared, we conclude that Sasha is well prepared.</text>
<logic>well_prepared(sasha)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established Sasha is well prepared, we can apply the rule from PREMISE_1. The rule tells us that well preparation directly leads to focus, so Sasha must be focused.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,1">
<text>Since Sasha is well prepared, the rule that well prepared implies focused lets us infer that Sasha is focused.</text>
<logic>focused(sasha)</logic>
</step>

With Sasha focused, the disjunction from PREMISE_5 ("not focused or impartial") cannot be satisfied by the first term, because Sasha is indeed focused. Therefore the second term must hold, and Sasha is impartial.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,5">
<text>With Sasha focused, the disjunction that either she is not focused or she is impartial forces Sasha to be impartial.</text>
<logic>impartial(sasha)</logic>
</step>

Having Sasha impartial allows us to invoke the rule in PREMISE_2, which says impartiality guarantees patience. Consequently, Sasha is patient.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,2">
<text>Because Sasha is impartial, the rule linking impartial to patient gives us that Sasha is patient.</text>
<logic>patient(sasha)</logic>
</step>

We now know Sasha is patient. The rule in PREMISE_3 says that if Sasha were proficient, she would be not patient. Since she is patient, the antecedent (proficient) must be false; otherwise we would have a contradiction. Hence we infer that Sasha is not proficient.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,3">
<text>Sasha being patient together with the rule that proficient would make her not patient lets us deduce that Sasha cannot be proficient.</text>
<logic>~proficient(sasha)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction in PREMISE_4 tells us Sasha is either proficient or judicious. We have just shown she cannot be proficient, so the only remaining possibility is that Sasha is judicious. This yields the desired conclusion.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,4">
<text>Given that Sasha is not proficient and the earlier disjunction that she is either proficient or judicious, we infer that Sasha is judicious.</text>
<logic>judicious(sasha)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Sasha is judicious, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: In the realm of the gods, Noel feels anxious. Stella shows her creative side, and Faith is known for being strategic. Whenever something is strategic, it is also courageous, so Faith is courageous because she is strategic. If someone is principled, then they are diligent, which means Noel would be diligent if he were principled. Preparedness leads to organization: any entity that is prepared becomes organized, so if Stella were prepared she would be organized. An anxious being becomes resourceful, so Noel would be resourceful because he is anxious. Determination brings support: any determined individual is supportive, so a determined Faith would be supportive. Active things cannot be organized; specifically, if Stella were active then she would not be organized. The myths also contain several alternatives: either Stella is not agile or she is prepared; either Stella is not creative or she is agile; either Faith is not courageous or Faith is determined; either Noel is not resourceful or Noel is principled.
examples.question: Is Stella active?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether Stella is active based on a collection of mythic facts and conditional statements. The goal is to see if the premises force Stella to be not active, which would make the query false.

The rule linking preparation to organization tells us that any entity that is prepared automatically becomes organized. This will be useful later when we establish Stella's preparation status.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Stella is prepared, then she becomes organized.</text>
<logic>organized(stella) :- prepared(stella)</logic>
</step>

The second rule states that if Stella were active, she could not be organized. This creates a direct conflict with any possibility of her being both active and organized, setting up a potential contradiction.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Stella is active, then she cannot be organized.</text>
<logic>~organized(stella) :- active(stella)</logic>
</step>

The first disjunction offers two alternatives: either Stella lacks agility or she is prepared. It provides a bridge between agility and preparation that we can exploit once we know something about her agility.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Either Stella is not agile or she is prepared.</text>
<logic>~agile(stella) v prepared(stella)</logic>
</step>

The second disjunction says that Stella is either not creative or she is agile. Since we already have a fact that Stella is creative, this forces the other side of the disjunction—her agility.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Either Stella is not creative or she is agile.</text>
<logic>~creative(stella) v agile(stella)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Noel is anxious is recorded for completeness, though it does not directly affect Stella's activity status. It supports other rules about resourcefulness, but those are not needed for the current query.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Noel is anxious.</text>
<logic>anxious(noel)</logic>
</step>

Faith being strategic is another factual detail that underpins other rules about courage and support, but it does not interact with Stella's properties in this proof.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Faith is strategic.</text>
<logic>strategic(faith)</logic>
</step>

Stella's creativity is explicitly given, which will interact with the disjunction about creativity versus agility, allowing us to deduce her agility.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Stella is creative.</text>
<logic>creative(stella)</logic>
</step>

Because Stella is creative, the disjunction "not creative or agile" cannot be satisfied by the first option; the creative clause is true, so the only way for the disjunction to hold is for Stella to be agile. Thus we infer agility for Stella.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,4">
<text>From Stella being creative and the disjunction that she is either not creative or agile, we infer that Stella must be agile.</text>
<logic>agile(stella)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have agility, we turn to the other disjunction "not agile or prepared". Since we have established that Stella is agile, the first alternative is false, leaving preparation as the only way to satisfy the disjunction. Consequently, Stella must be prepared.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,3">
<text>Using Stella's agility together with the disjunction that she is either not agile or prepared, we conclude that Stella is prepared.</text>
<logic>prepared(stella)</logic>
</step>

With Stella prepared, we can apply the rule from Premise 1 that preparation leads to organization. This gives us the conclusion that Stella is organized.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,1">
<text>Because Stella is prepared and the rule that prepared implies organized, we deduce that Stella is organized.</text>
<logic>organized(stella)</logic>
</step>

We now have Stella organized, but Premise 2 tells us that if Stella were active she would be not organized. Since she is organized, the condition for being active leads to a contradiction, forcing the negation of activity: Stella cannot be active.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,2">
<text>Given that Stella is organized and the rule that active implies not organized, we infer that Stella cannot be active.</text>
<logic>~active(stella)</logic>
</step>

We derived Stella is not active, so the conclusion Stella is active is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: In the library, librarian Arden is known to be empathetic. Whenever someone is empathetic, that person is also responsible, so Arden's empathy guarantees her responsibility. Moreover, any responsible person in the library is automatically focused, meaning Arden's responsibility makes her focused. Finally, the library policy states that if a staff member is principled, then that person is not focused. These are the only facts and rules we have about Arden.
examples.question: Is librarian Arden principled?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to decide whether librarian Arden can be described as principled. The goal is to see if the available facts and rules force Arden to be principled or to rule it out.

The first rule tells us that responsibility brings focus. This connection will be useful later because if we can show Arden is responsible, we will automatically get that she is focused.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule that being responsible makes Arden focused.</text>
<logic>focused(librarian_arden) :- responsible(librarian_arden)</logic>
</step>

The second rule links empathy to responsibility. Since we already know Arden is empathetic, this rule gives us a pathway to obtain her responsibility status.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule that being empathetic makes Arden responsible.</text>
<logic>responsible(librarian_arden) :- empathetic(librarian_arden)</logic>
</step>

The third rule states that principled people are not focused. This contrapositive relationship will allow us to use Arden's focus status to argue against her being principled.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule that being principled means Arden is not focused.</text>
<logic>~focused(librarian_arden) :- principled(librarian_arden)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Arden is empathetic is the starting point of our chain of deductions. It directly activates the empathy‑to‑responsibility rule.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>It is given that Arden is empathetic.</text>
<logic>empathetic(librarian_arden)</logic>
</step>

Applying the empathy‑to‑responsibility rule to the known fact that Arden is empathetic yields that Arden must be responsible. This is a straightforward use of Modus Ponens: the antecedent holds, so the consequent follows.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>From Arden being empathetic and the rule that empathy leads to responsibility, we infer that Arden is responsible.</text>
<logic>responsible(librarian_arden)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established Arden's responsibility, we can invoke the responsibility‑to‑focus rule. Because a responsible person is focused, we conclude that Arden is indeed focused.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>Since Arden is now known to be responsible and responsibility leads to focus, we derive that Arden is focused.</text>
<logic>focused(librarian_arden)</logic>
</step>

We have derived that Arden is focused, but the rule about principled individuals says a principled person would be not focused. Using Modus Tollens, the presence of focus contradicts the condition for being principled, so we infer that Arden cannot be principled.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">
<text>Given that Arden is focused and that being principled would imply not focused, we conclude that Arden cannot be principled.</text>
<logic>~principled(librarian_arden)</logic>
</step>

We derived Librarian_arden is not principled, so the conclusion Librarian_arden is principled is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.question: Is Paul clever?

The disjunction offers two possibilities: either Paul lacks knowledge or he is grateful. When we later learn Paul is indeed knowledgeable, the only way for the statement to remain true is for the other alternative—Paul being grateful—to hold.

<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,3">

<logic>knowledgeable(paul)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know Paul is knowledgeable, the disjunction "not knowledgeable or grateful" cannot be satisfied by the first option, because the first option is false. Therefore, to keep the disjunction true, the second option must be true, and we conclude Paul is grateful.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,6">

<logic>grateful(paul)</logic>
</step>

Having established Paul is grateful, we invoke the rule that gratefulness leads to friendliness. By applying modus ponens again, we obtain that Paul is friendly, moving us further along the chain toward cleverness.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,2">

With Paul now friendly, the rule stating that friendliness guarantees punctuality lets us infer Paul is punctual. This step brings us one link closer to rationality.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,4">

<logic>punctual(paul)</logic>
</step>

Since Paul is punctual, the conditional that punctuality yields rationality allows us to deduce Paul is rational. This is the pivotal fact needed for the final cleverness inference.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,5">

<logic>rational(paul)</logic>

examples.question: Is Mia disciplined?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether Mia possesses the property of being disciplined. The available information includes a fact about Mia's decisiveness and two conditional rules linking discipline, curiosity, and decisiveness.

The first rule tells us that discipline entails curiosity; this will be useful later because if we can show Mia is not curious, we can work backwards to say she cannot be disciplined. Remembering this conditional relationship sets up the later contrapositive reasoning.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Mia is disciplined then Mia is curious.</text>
<logic>curious(mia) :- disciplined(mia)</logic>
</step>

The second rule states that curiosity prevents decisiveness. This connects directly to the known fact that Mia is decisive, allowing us to use modus tollens to eliminate the possibility that she is curious.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Mia is curious then Mia is not decisive.</text>
<logic>~decisive(mia) :- curious(mia)</logic>
</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Mia is decisive.</text>
<logic>decisive(mia)</logic>
</step>

We know Mia is decisive (Premise 3). If she were curious, the second rule would force her to be not decisive, which contradicts the fact. Therefore, by modus tollens, Mia cannot be curious. This step removes curiosity from Mia's possible traits, clearing the way for the next inference.
<step n="4" type="derived" from="3,2">
<text>Because Mia is decisive and curiosity would make her not decisive, we infer that Mia is not curious.</text>
<logic>~curious(mia)</logic>
</step>

We have just established that Mia is not curious. The first rule says that being disciplined would guarantee curiosity. Since Mia lacks curiosity, the contrapositive of that rule tells us she cannot be disciplined. Applying modus tollens again yields the conclusion that Mia is not disciplined.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,1">
<text>Since Mia is not curious and discipline would imply curiosity, we conclude that Mia is not disciplined.</text>
<logic>~disciplined(mia)</logic>
</step>

We derived Mia is not disciplined, so the conclusion Mia is disciplined is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.question: Is Kendall adaptable?

We need to determine whether Kendall possesses the adaptable property. The goal is to trace a chain of logical deductions from the supplied facts and rules that leads to adaptable(kendall).

<step n="2" type="premise">

This rule tells us that energy translates into meticulousness for Kendall. Once we have energetic(kendall), we can immediately infer meticulous(kendall), which will later help us reject enthusiasm.

The rule states that enthusiasm would cause a lack of meticulousness. By later showing Kendall is meticulous, we will be able to use the contrapositive to rule out enthusiasm.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Kendall is enthusiastic, then Kendall is not meticulous.</text>
<logic>~meticulous(kendall) :- enthusiastic(kendall)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction provides two alternatives for Kendall: either enthusiastic or adaptable. If we can eliminate the enthusiastic option, the adaptable alternative must hold.

The fact that the lake is healthy is recorded for completeness, though it does not play a direct role in the adaptable derivation.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>The fact tells us that the lake is healthy.</text>
<logic>healthy(lake)</logic>
</step>

Kendall being proficient is a central fact; it activates the chain of rules that eventually leads to adaptability.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>The fact tells us that Kendall is proficient.</text>
<logic>proficient(kendall)</logic>
</step>

Applying modus ponens to the fact proficient(kendall) and the rule that proficient implies evidence‑driven, we obtain evidence_driven(kendall). This gives us the first needed intermediate property.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,2">

Now that we have evidence_driven(kendall), we use the rule from PREMISE_1: evidence‑driven leads to energetic. Modus ponens yields energetic(kendall), moving us closer to meticulousness.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,1">

<logic>energetic(kendall)</logic>

With energetic(kendall) established, PREMISE_3 allows us to infer meticulous(kendall). This step is crucial because it sets up the condition that contradicts enthusiasm.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,3">

<logic>meticulous(kendall)</logic>

We know from STEP_10 that Kendall is meticulous. PREMISE_4 tells us that if Kendall were enthusiastic, Kendall would be not meticulous. Since Kendall is indeed meticulous, the only way to avoid a contradiction is to conclude that Kendall is not enthusiastic. This is an application of modus tollens.

Having shown Kendall is not enthusiastic, we return to the disjunction in PREMISE_5. With one disjunct (enthusiastic) falsified, the remaining disjunct must be true, so Kendall is adaptable. This is a classic disjunctive syllogism.

examples.context: Cleo is knowledgeable. Whenever someone is knowledgeable, that person is also active. If a person is honest, then that person is alert. Being witty guarantees that the person is courageous. Any analytical individual is honest. Whenever someone is alert, they become optimistic. If someone is active, they are witty as well. Productive people are never courageous. Finally, Cleo is either productive or analytical, but we do not know which one holds.
examples.question: Is Cleo optimistic?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to determine whether Cleo enjoys optimism based on the supplied knowledge about her traits and the logical connections among them. The goal is to trace a chain of implications from the known fact that she is knowledgeable to the desired property of optimism.

This premise tells us that knowledge automatically grants activity. It will be useful later because we already know Cleo is knowledgeable, allowing us to start the chain of reasoning.

Here we learn that honesty guarantees alertness. Although we do not yet know Cleo is honest, this rule will become relevant once we can derive honesty from other properties.

</step>

The third rule links wit to courage. It will help us later when we obtain wit, enabling us to infer courage and then use the productivity‑courage relationship.

<text>The rule that wit leads to courage: if someone is witty, then they are courageous.</text>
<logic>courageous(cleo) :- witty(cleo)</logic>

This rule states that being analytical ensures honesty. It will be crucial after we deduce that Cleo is analytical, giving us a path to honesty and then alertness.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The rule that being analytical implies honesty: if someone is analytical, then they are honest.</text>

The fifth premise provides the direct bridge from alertness to optimism, which is the final step we need to reach the query.
<step n="5" type="premise">

<logic>optimistic(cleo) :- alert(cleo)</logic>
</step>

This rule says activity leads to wit, closing the loop between the first fact (knowledge) and the third rule (wit → courage).
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>The rule that activity brings wit: if someone is active, then they are witty.</text>
<logic>witty(cleo) :- active(cleo)</logic>
</step>

The seventh premise tells us that productivity forces a lack of courage. It will allow us to eliminate the possibility that Cleo is productive once we have established her courage.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>The rule that productivity prevents courage: if someone is productive, then they are not courageous.</text>
<logic>~courageous(cleo) :- productive(cleo)</logic>

The disjunction gives us two alternatives for Cleo: she is either productive or analytical. By ruling out one alternative, we will be forced to accept the other.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>The disjunction stating that Cleo is either productive or analytical.</text>
<logic>productive(cleo) v analytical(cleo)</logic>
</step>

The only explicit fact we start with: Cleo possesses knowledge. This anchors the entire deduction.
<step n="9" type="premise">
<text>The fact that Cleo is knowledgeable.</text>
<logic>knowledgeable(cleo)</logic>
</step>

Because we know Cleo is knowledgeable and we have the rule that knowledge implies activity, we can directly infer that Cleo is active. This gives us the first new property needed for later steps.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,1">
<text>From Cleo being knowledgeable and the knowledge‑to‑activity rule, we infer that Cleo is active.</text>
<logic>active(cleo)</logic>
</step>

Having established Cleo's activity, we apply the rule that activity yields wit. Consequently, Cleo becomes witty, which is the prerequisite for the next inference about courage.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,6">
<text>Since Cleo is active and activity implies wit, we conclude that Cleo is witty.</text>
<logic>witty(cleo)</logic>

With Cleo now witty, we use the rule that wit leads to courage. This makes Cleo courageous, a fact that will interact with the productivity‑courage rule.

Since courageous individuals cannot be the result of productivity (productivity would make someone not courageous), the presence of courage lets us conclude that Cleo cannot be productive. This eliminates one side of the earlier disjunction.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,7">
<text>Given that courageous people cannot be produced by productivity (productivity implies not courageous), we infer that Cleo cannot be productive.</text>
<logic>~productive(cleo)</logic>

The disjunction told us Cleo is either productive or analytical. Because we have just shown she is not productive, the only remaining possibility is that she is analytical. Hence we deduce her analyticity.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,8">

Now that Cleo is analytical, the rule linking analyticity to honesty gives us that Cleo is honest. This moves us closer to alertness.

examples.context: In the winery, Jordan is known to be clever. Dawn, on the other hand, is resourceful. Another guideline says that if Dawn is ambitious, then she becomes organized. Likewise, the team believes that being resourceful guarantees that Dawn is supportive. The records also note a logical alternative: either Jordan is not clever or Jordan is cautious. Similarly, there is an alternative concerning Dawn: either she is not supportive or she is ambitious.
examples.question: Is Jordan diplomatic?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether Jordan is diplomatic based on the information provided about the winery staff. To answer, we will use the given facts, rules, and logical alternatives to derive Jordan's diplomatic status.

The conditional linking cautious to diplomatic is crucial because it provides the bridge from a possible property of Jordan to the target property. Keeping this rule in mind will allow us to turn a derived cautiousness into diplomaticness later.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Jordan is cautious, then Jordan is diplomatic.</text>
<logic>diplomatic(jordan) :- cautious(jordan)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction gives us two possibilities concerning Jordan: either he lacks cleverness or he is cautious. Since we already know Jordan is clever, this premise will help us eliminate one option and focus on the other.
<step n="2" type="premise">

<logic>~clever(jordan) v cautious(jordan)</logic>

Stating that Dawn is resourceful supplies a fact that will later trigger the rule making Dawn supportive, though it is not directly needed for Jordan's diplomatic status. It nonetheless completes the set of premises required by the problem statement.

<logic>resourceful(dawn)</logic>
</step>

Jordan being clever is a concrete fact that directly interacts with the disjunction from PREMISE_2. Knowing he is clever will allow us to apply disjunctive syllogism to obtain a new fact about Jordan.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Jordan is clever.</text>
<logic>clever(jordan)</logic>
</step>

Because Jordan is clever, the first part of the disjunction (~clever(jordan)) is false, forcing the second part (cautious(jordan)) to be true by disjunctive syllogism. This gives us the needed cautious property for Jordan, which is the antecedent of the rule in PREMISE_1.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>From Jordan being clever and the disjunction, we infer that Jordan must be cautious.</text>
<logic>cautious(jordan)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established Jordan is cautious, we can invoke the rule from PREMISE_1: cautious implies diplomatic. Applying modus ponens yields the conclusion that Jordan is diplomatic.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">

examples.context: The vintner is currently fermenting. Whenever a wine is fermenting, it is certified. Being certified guarantees that the wine follows a classic style. If a wine is of classic style and there is no abnormal oak‑age, then it becomes oak‑aged. If a wine is oak‑aged and there is no abnormal alert, then it is ready to be bottled. Moreover, there is no evidence of any abnormal oak‑age or abnormal alert affecting the vintner’s wine.
examples.question: Is it not the case that the vintner’s wine is ready to be bottled?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether the vintner’s wine is not ready to be bottled. To answer, we must examine the chain of conditions that start from the known fact that the vintner is fermenting and see whether they lead to the wine being ready to be bottled.

This premise tells us that fermentation guarantees certification. It is the first link that will allow us to move from the known fermenting state toward the later properties.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If a wine is fermenting, then it is certified.</text>
<logic>certified(vintner) :- fermenting(vintner)</logic>
</step>

Here we learn that certification brings about a classic style. This rule will be used after we have established certification to reach the classic style condition needed for the defaults.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If a wine is certified, then it follows a classic style.</text>
<logic>classic_style(vintner) :- certified(vintner)</logic>
</step>

The default rule states that an oak‑aged wine, provided there is no abnormal alert, becomes ready to be bottled. This will be the final step that yields the ready‑to‑bottle status once we have oak‑age.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If a wine is oak‑aged and there is no abnormal alert, then it is ready to be bottled.</text>
<logic>ready_to_bottle(vintner) :- oak_aged(vintner), not ab_ready_to_bottle(vintner)</logic>
</step>

This default says that a classic‑style wine, assuming no abnormal oak‑age, becomes oak‑aged. It connects the classic style we will obtain to the oak‑aged property needed for the previous rule.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If a wine is of classic style and there is no abnormal oak‑age, then it is oak‑aged.</text>
<logic>oak_aged(vintner) :- classic_style(vintner), not ab_oak_aged(vintner)</logic>
</step>

The fact that the vintner is fermenting gives us the starting point for the whole reasoning chain.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The vintner is fermenting.</text>
<logic>fermenting(vintner)</logic>
</step>

Using the fact that the vintner is fermenting together with the rule that fermentation implies certification, we can confidently assert that the vintner is certified. This application of modus ponens moves us one step forward in the chain.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>From the fact that the vintner is fermenting and the rule that fermentation implies certification, we infer that the vintner is certified.</text>
<logic>certified(vintner)</logic>
</step>

Having established certification, we now apply the rule that certification leads to classic style. This yields the classic style property for the vintner’s wine, setting up the condition required by the oak‑age default.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">
<text>Since the vintner is certified, and certification implies classic style, we conclude the vintner’s wine follows a classic style.</text>
<logic>classic_style(vintner)</logic>
</step>

With classic style in hand and knowing that there is no abnormal oak‑age, the default rule gives us oak‑age. Then, because we also have no abnormal alert, the next default tells us that an oak‑aged wine becomes ready to be bottled. Thus, by chaining the two defaults, we derive that the wine is ready to be bottled.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,4,3">
<text>Given the classic style, the default that classic style leads to oak‑age (absent abnormal oak‑age), and the default that oak‑age leads to ready‑to‑bottle (absent abnormal alert), we derive that the wine is ready to be bottled.</text>
<logic>ready_to_bottle(vintner)</logic>
</step>

We derived Vintner is ready to bottle, so the conclusion Vintner is not ready to bottle is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: Bellamy is curious, and Phoebe is open-minded. Angel possesses the quality of being perceptive. Whenever Angel is meticulous, Angel is also adaptable. Whenever Bellamy is strategic, Bellamy becomes friendly. If Bellamy is curious, then Bellamy is goal-oriented. Either Angel is not diplomatic or Angel is meticulous. Either Bellamy is not goal-oriented or Bellamy is strategic.
examples.question: Is Phoebe capable?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to decide whether Phoebe possesses the property of being capable. The goal is to see if the available facts and rules force Phoebe to be incapable, which would make the query false.

This premise links Phoebe's capability to her cooperativeness; it will be useful because later we will derive something about Phoebe's cooperativeness and then work backwards to capability.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Phoebe were capable, she would also be cooperative.</text>
<logic>cooperative(phoebe) :- capable(phoebe)</logic>
</step>

This premise tells us that cooperativeness would contradict Phoebe's known open‑mindedness. It creates a tension that we can exploit to rule out cooperativeness.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Phoebe were cooperative, she would cease to be open‑minded.</text>
<logic>~open_minded(phoebe) :- cooperative(phoebe)</logic>
</step>

Bellamy's curiosity is recorded, though it does not directly affect Phoebe, it is part of the full knowledge base that the context must contain.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>It is given that Bellamy is curious.</text>
<logic>curious(bellamy)</logic>
</step>

Angel's perceptiveness is noted, again part of the complete set of facts, ensuring the context is exhaustive.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>It is given that Angel is perceptive.</text>
<logic>perceptive(angel)</logic>
</step>

Phoebe being open‑minded is a concrete fact that will clash with the conditional that a cooperative Phoebe would not be open‑minded.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>It is given that Phoebe is open‑minded.</text>
<logic>open_minded(phoebe)</logic>
</step>

Since Phoebe is open‑minded, the conditional "if cooperative then not open‑minded" cannot hold; otherwise we would have both open‑minded and not open‑minded simultaneously. To avoid this contradiction, we must conclude that Phoebe is not cooperative. This is a classic use of modus tollens: the consequent is false, so the antecedent must also be false.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,2">
<text>From Phoebe being open‑minded and the conditional that a cooperative Phoebe would be not open‑minded, we infer that Phoebe cannot be cooperative.</text>

</step>

Having established that Phoebe is not cooperative, we turn to the earlier rule linking capability to cooperativeness. If Phoebe were capable, she would be cooperative, but we just derived that she is not cooperative. Applying modus tollens again, we infer that Phoebe cannot be capable.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,1">
<text>From the conclusion that Phoebe is not cooperative and the conditional that a capable Phoebe would be cooperative, we infer that Phoebe cannot be capable.</text>
<logic>~capable(phoebe)</logic>
</step>

We derived Phoebe is not capable, so the conclusion Phoebe is capable is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: In the marine biology research team, Dean exhibits several traits. Dean is responsible. Whenever Dean is content, he is also focused. If Dean is reflective, then Dean is not responsible. Moreover, Dean is either reflective or content. No other defaults are given.
examples.question: Is Dean focused according to the given information?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to determine whether Dean is focused. The goal is to use the given facts and conditional statements to infer his focus status.

The first rule tells us that content implies focus for Dean. This will be useful later when we establish Dean's content status.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule that if Dean is content then Dean is focused.</text>
<logic>focused(dean) :- content(dean)</logic>
</step>

The second rule states that if Dean were reflective, he would not be responsible. This relationship will help us eliminate the reflective possibility.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule that if Dean is reflective then Dean is not responsible.</text>
<logic>~responsible(dean) :- reflective(dean)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction informs us that Dean must be either reflective or content, providing two exclusive possibilities. Knowing one of these must hold will allow us to narrow down his actual property.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Dean is either reflective or content.</text>
<logic>reflective(dean) v content(dean)</logic>
</step>

We are told directly that Dean is responsible. This fact will interact with the reflective‑responsible rule.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Dean is responsible.</text>
<logic>responsible(dean)</logic>
</step>

Since Dean is known to be responsible, and the rule says a reflective Dean would be not responsible, the only way both statements can be true is if Dean is not reflective. Thus we infer the negation of reflective for Dean.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>Since Dean is responsible and a reflective Dean would not be responsible, we infer that Dean is not reflective.</text>
<logic>~reflective(dean)</logic>
</step>

We have established that Dean is not reflective, and we also know from the disjunction that he is either reflective or content. By removing the impossible reflective option, the remaining alternative is that Dean must be content.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,3">
<text>Given Dean is not reflective and that he must be either reflective or content, we conclude Dean is content.</text>
<logic>content(dean)</logic>
</step>

Having concluded that Dean is content, we can now apply the rule that content leads to focus. Consequently, Dean is focused.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,1">
<text>Because Dean is content and any content Dean is focused, we deduce Dean is focused.</text>
<logic>focused(dean)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Dean is focused, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: Nutritionist Maya is evaluating Cameron's dietary mindset. She notes that Cameron is perceptive about food choices. Maya also knows that whenever someone is motivated, they become witty in the kitchen, so any motivated person is witty. In contrast, whenever someone is energetic, they lose their wittiness, meaning an energetic person is not witty. Maya further records that either Cameron is not inventive or he is motivated, and that either Cameron is not perceptive or he is inventive. These observations give a complete picture of Cameron's traits for the analysis.
examples.question: Given the information above, can we conclude that Cameron is not energetic?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether Cameron can be classified as not energetic based on the supplied nutritional personality facts and rules. The goal is to chain the given premises together until we reach a statement about Cameron's energetic status.

This premise tells us the direct link between motivation and wittiness, which will become useful later when we establish Cameron's motivation. Recognizing that motivation yields wittiness sets up a forward‑chaining path toward the query.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Cameron is motivated then he is witty.</text>
<logic>witty(cameron) :- motivated(cameron)</logic>
</step>

Here we have the conditional that energetic people lose their wittiness. This rule is crucial because it provides a way to infer the absence of energy from the presence of wittiness, which is the reverse direction we need for the final step.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Cameron is energetic then he is not witty.</text>
<logic>~witty(cameron) :- energetic(cameron)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction gives two possibilities: either Cameron lacks inventiveness or he is motivated. This will allow us to eliminate one option once we know something about his inventiveness, steering us toward motivation.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Either Cameron is not inventive or Cameron is motivated.</text>
<logic>~inventive(cameron) v motivated(cameron)</logic>
</step>

This second disjunction offers a choice between not being perceptive or being inventive. Since we already have a fact about Cameron's perceptiveness, we can use this to resolve the disjunction and obtain inventiveness.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Either Cameron is not perceptive or Cameron is inventive.</text>
<logic>~perceptive(cameron) v inventive(cameron)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Cameron is perceptive directly contradicts the "not perceptive" side of the previous disjunction. Knowing this fact is the key to applying disjunctive syllogism and moving forward.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>It is a fact that Cameron is perceptive.</text>
<logic>perceptive(cameron)</logic>
</step>

Because Cameron is perceptive, the "not perceptive" alternative in the disjunction is false, leaving the only viable option that Cameron is inventive. This deduction follows the standard disjunctive syllogism pattern, turning a vague either/or into a concrete fact.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,4">
<text>From Cameron being perceptive and the disjunction "not perceptive or inventive", we infer that Cameron is inventive.</text>
<logic>inventive(cameron)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established Cameron's inventiveness, the disjunction "not inventive or motivated" can be resolved similarly: the "not inventive" part is false, so we must accept that Cameron is motivated. This step again uses disjunctive syllogism to convert the remaining disjunct into a definite statement.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">
<text>Using the fact that Cameron is inventive together with the disjunction "not inventive or motivated", we deduce that Cameron is motivated.</text>
<logic>motivated(cameron)</logic>
</step>

With motivation confirmed, we can apply the earlier rule that motivation guarantees wittiness. Therefore, Cameron inherits the witty property, linking his motivational state to a behavioral trait relevant for the next inference.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,1">
<text>Since Cameron is motivated, the rule "motivated implies witty" lets us conclude that Cameron is witty.</text>
<logic>witty(cameron)</logic>
</step>

We now have Cameron being witty, and we also know that if someone were energetic they would not be witty. By contraposition (modus tollens), the presence of wittiness forces the negation of energeticness, allowing us to conclude that Cameron is not energetic.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,2">
<text>Because Cameron is witty and the rule "energetic implies not witty" holds, we infer that Cameron is not energetic.</text>
<logic>~energetic(cameron)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Cameron is not energetic, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

Also, either Avery is not steady or Avery is hardy. Finally, either Felix is not docile or Felix is active.
examples.question: Based on the information given, can we conclude that Avery is not hardy?
examples.completion:

We are asked to decide whether Avery lacks hardiness. The premises give us information about Avery's alertness, steadiness, and a chain of conditionals that connect these properties to hardiness. Our goal is to see if the available statements allow us to assert that Avery is not hardy.

The first disjunction tells us that at least one of two possibilities holds: either Avery is not alert or Avery is steady. This sets up a choice that will be resolved once we know Avery's alert status. It is an essential piece for applying disjunctive reasoning later.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>Either Avery is not alert or Avery is steady.</text>
<logic>~alert(avery) v steady(avery)</logic>
</step>

The second disjunction offers another pair of alternatives: either Avery is not steady or Avery is hardy. Together with the result of the first disjunction, this will let us infer Avery's hardiness if we can eliminate the negative alternative.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>Either Avery is not steady or Avery is hardy.</text>
<logic>~steady(avery) v hardy(avery)</logic>
</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Felix has been trained.</text>
<logic>trained(felix)</logic>
</step>

<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Avery is alert.</text>
<logic>alert(avery)</logic>
</step>

We already know from the facts that Avery is alert. Because the disjunction says "not alert or steady," the "not alert" part cannot be true. Disjunctive syllogism therefore lets us drop that option and accept the remaining one: Avery is steady. This step translates the abstract logical rule into a concrete property of Avery.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,1">
<text>Since Avery is alert, the alternative that Avery is not alert must be false, leaving Avery steady.</text>
<logic>steady(avery)</logic>
</step>

Having established that Avery is steady, we turn to the second disjunction. The clause "not steady" is now false, so the only way for the disjunction to hold is for the other clause, "hardy," to be true. Thus, we conclude that Avery is hardy, directly following from the previous step and the second disjunction.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,2">
<text>Because Avery is steady, the alternative that Avery is not steady is false, so Avery must be hardy.</text>
<logic>hardy(avery)</logic>
</step>

We derived Avery is hardy, so the conclusion Avery is not hardy is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: In the chess scenario, the bishop is aggressive. Any piece that is positional is also strategic. Moreover, if a piece is strategic, then it is not aggressive. These statements capture all the given facts and rules for the bishop.
examples.question: Is the bishop positional?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether the bishop has a positional style. To answer this, we will use the given facts about aggressiveness and the conditional relationships between positional, strategic, and aggressiveness.

The rule that any positional piece is strategic is important because it links the property we are trying to prove (positional) to another property (strategic) that appears in other rules. Keeping this connection in mind will allow us to reason backwards from what we know about the bishop.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>Any piece that is positional is also strategic.</text>
<logic>strategic(bishop) :- positional(bishop)</logic>
</step>

The second rule tells us that being strategic forces a piece to be non‑aggressive. Since we already know the bishop is aggressive, this rule will help us eliminate the possibility that the bishop is strategic, and thus affect the positional claim.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If a piece is strategic, then it is not aggressive.</text>
<logic>~aggressive(bishop) :- strategic(bishop)</logic>
</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The bishop is aggressive.</text>
<logic>aggressive(bishop)</logic>
</step>

We start with the fact that the bishop is aggressive. The rule that a strategic piece must be non‑aggressive contradicts this fact if the bishop were strategic. By modus tollens, because the bishop is aggressive, it cannot be strategic, so we derive that the bishop is not strategic.
<step n="4" type="derived" from="3,2">
<text>Since the bishop is aggressive, and being strategic would make it not aggressive, we can infer that the bishop cannot be strategic.</text>
<logic>~strategic(bishop)</logic>
</step>

Now we have that the bishop is not strategic. The earlier rule states that any positional piece must be strategic. Applying modus tollens again, the lack of strategic status means the bishop cannot be positional. Hence we infer that the bishop is not positional.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,1">
<text>Because the bishop is not strategic, and being positional would make it strategic, we conclude the bishop is not positional.</text>
<logic>~positional(bishop)</logic>
</step>

We derived Bishop is not positional, so the conclusion Bishop is positional is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: Ember is perceptive. Whenever something is perceptive, it is also intuitive, so Ember is intuitive. examples.question: Based on the information provided, can we conclude that Ember is astute?

We are asked to determine if Ember possesses the property astute. To answer, we must see whether the given facts and conditional statements allow us to derive astuteness for Ember.

Here we have a bridge from rationality to gratefulness. If we can show Ember is rational, the previous rule will let us infer dedicatedness, moving us forward in the chain of properties.
<step n="2" type="premise">

<logic>grateful(ember) :- rational(ember)</logic>

This premise converts Ember's perceptiveness into intuitiveness. Since we already know Ember is perceptive, we can immediately obtain the intuitive property, which will trigger further implications.

The conditional from rested to punctual lets us turn a rested status into punctuality. Later, when we derive restedness, we will be able to claim Ember is punctual, unless another rule contradicts it.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If Ember is rested, then Ember is punctual.</text>
<logic>punctual(ember) :- rested(ember)</logic>
</step>

Restedness follows from intuitiveness, so once we have Ember intuitive (from PREMISE_3), we can infer Ember rested. This creates a cascade: perceptive → intuitive → rested → punctual.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If Ember is intuitive, then Ember is rested.</text>
<logic>rested(ember) :- intuitive(ember)</logic>
</step>

The rule about cautiousness creates a negative punctuality conclusion. Should we ever be able to assert Ember is cautious, we would have to conclude Ember is not punctual, which would clash with the punctuality derived from the rested chain.
<step n="6" type="premise">

The explicit fact that Ember is perceptive is the starting point for the forward reasoning. It activates PREMISE_3, allowing us to generate the intuitive property and continue the chain of deductions.

<logic>perceptive(ember)</logic>
</step>

<step n="8" type="derived">

examples.context: Derek is brave. Shay is friendly. Olga is scholarly. If Derek is brave, then Derek is adaptable.
