examples.context: Captain Elena is known to be responsive. Whenever a pilot is ready, that pilot is also methodical. If a pilot is responsive, then the pilot is evidence_oriented. Any pilot who is keen is automatically fully_ready. Being alert guarantees that the pilot is not evidence_oriented. Either Elena is alert or Elena is ready. Either Elena is not methodical or Elena is keen.
examples.question: Is it false that Elena is fully_ready?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether it is false that Elena is fully_ready. To answer, we will follow the chain of information given about her qualities and the logical connections between them. The goal is to see if the premises force Elena to be fully_ready.

This premise links the property of being ready to being methodical, meaning any pilot who is ready automatically satisfies the methodical condition. It will become useful once we know whether Elena is ready.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule that any ready pilot is also methodical.</text>
<logic>methodical(elena) :- ready(elena)</logic>
</step>

Here we are told that responsiveness guarantees the evidence_oriented attribute. Since we already have Elena as responsive, this rule will let us infer a new fact about her.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule that any responsive pilot is evidence_oriented.</text>
<logic>evidence_oriented(elena) :- responsive(elena)</logic>
</step>

The third rule tells us that keenness leads directly to being fully_ready. When we later establish that Elena is keen, this rule will give us the sought‑after conclusion.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule that any keen pilot is fully_ready.</text>
<logic>fully_ready(elena) :- keen(elena)</logic>
</step>

This rule states that alertness precludes being evidence_oriented. It will let us eliminate the possibility that Elena is alert once we know she is evidence_oriented.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The rule that any alert pilot is not evidence_oriented.</text>
<logic>~evidence_oriented(elena) :- alert(elena)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction offers two mutually exclusive possibilities for Elena: either she is alert or she is ready. This will allow us to pinpoint her exact status once one of the alternatives is ruled out.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The statement that Elena is either alert or ready.</text>
<logic>alert(elena) v ready(elena)</logic>
</step>

The second disjunction says that either Elena is not methodical or she is keen. This will become decisive after we establish her methodical status.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>The statement that either Elena is not methodical or she is keen.</text>
<logic>~methodical(elena) v keen(elena)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Elena is responsive is the starting point of the derivation. It directly activates the rule that turns responsiveness into evidence_oriented.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>The fact that Elena is responsive.</text>
<logic>responsive(elena)</logic>
</step>

Using the fact that Elena is responsive together with the rule that responsive pilots are evidence_oriented, we can conclude that Elena is evidence_oriented. This is a straightforward application of modus ponens.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,2">
<text>From Elena being responsive we infer that she is evidence_oriented.</text>
<logic>evidence_oriented(elena)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know Elena is evidence_oriented, the rule stating that alert pilots are not evidence_oriented lets us infer that Elena cannot be alert. This is a classic instance of modus tollens: if alert implied not evidence_oriented, the presence of evidence_oriented forces the negation of alert.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,4">
<text>Because Elena is evidence_oriented and alert pilots are never evidence_oriented, we conclude that Elena cannot be alert.</text>
<logic>~alert(elena)</logic>
</step>

Having established that Elena is not alert, the disjunction ‘alert or ready’ leaves only the ready alternative viable. Therefore, Elena must be ready. This follows from disjunctive syllogism, discarding the false disjunct.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,5">
<text>Since Elena is not alert and the disjunction says she is either alert or ready, we deduce that she must be ready.</text>
<logic>ready(elena)</logic>
</step>

Since Elena is ready, the ready‑to‑methodical rule applies, giving us that Elena is methodical. This is another direct modus ponens step.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,1">
<text>From Elena being ready we apply the ready‑to‑methodical rule and obtain that she is methodical.</text>
<logic>methodical(elena)</logic>
</step>

With Elena now known to be methodical, the disjunction ‘not methodical or keen’ forces the second option, because the first option is false. Consequently, Elena is keen, derived by disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,6">
<text>Given that Elena is methodical, the disjunction ‘not methodical or keen’ forces Elena to be keen.</text>
<logic>keen(elena)</logic>
</step>

Because Elena is keen, the keen‑to‑fully_ready rule tells us that she is fully_ready. This final modus ponens gives us the exact property the query is concerned with.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,3">
<text>Because Elena is keen, the keen‑to‑fully_ready rule yields that she is fully_ready.</text>
<logic>fully_ready(elena)</logic>
</step>

We derived Elena is fully ready, so the conclusion Elena is not fully ready is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: In this study we consider the organic molecule cedar_alkene. It is known that cedar_alkene has a conjugated system. Whenever a molecule has a conjugated system, it is stable. Any molecule that is stable is also reactive. Being reactive guarantees that the molecule is bioactive. No other alternatives or defaults are mentioned.
examples.question: Is cedar_alkene bioactive?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to decide whether cedar_alkene possesses bioactivity based on the information provided. The goal is to follow the logical chain from its structural feature to the property of interest.

The first conditional tells us that a conjugated system leads to stability, which is the initial link in our chain. Recognizing this relationship is essential because stability is the next property we must establish.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule that if cedar_alkene has a conjugated system, then it is stable.</text>
<logic>is_stable(cedar_alkene) :- has_conjugated_system(cedar_alkene)</logic>
</step>

The second rule connects stability to reactivity, moving the argument forward one step. This link will allow us to translate the derived stability into a reactive characteristic.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule that if cedar_alkene is stable, then it is reactive.</text>
<logic>is_reactive(cedar_alkene) :- is_stable(cedar_alkene)</logic>
</step>

The third conditional bridges reactivity to bioactivity, the final property we care about. Understanding this connection ensures that once we have reactivity, we can immediately infer bioactivity.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule that if cedar_alkene is reactive, then it is bioactive.</text>
<logic>is_bioactive(cedar_alkene) :- is_reactive(cedar_alkene)</logic>
</step>

We are explicitly told that cedar_alkene has a conjugated system, giving us a concrete starting point. This fact activates the first rule and starts the cascade of deductions.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>It is given that cedar_alkene has a conjugated system.</text>
<logic>has_conjugated_system(cedar_alkene)</logic>
</step>

Applying modus ponens, the fact that cedar_alkene has a conjugated system together with the first rule yields that it is stable. This derived stability is now available for the next inference.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,1">
<text>From the fact that cedar_alkene has a conjugated system and the first rule, we infer that cedar_alkene is stable.</text>
<logic>is_stable(cedar_alkene)</logic>
</step>

With stability established, we again use modus ponens on the second rule, concluding that cedar_alkene is reactive. This new reactive status is the prerequisite for the final step.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,2">
<text>Using the newly derived stability and the second rule, we conclude that cedar_alkene is reactive.</text>
<logic>is_reactive(cedar_alkene)</logic>
</step>

Finally, invoking modus ponens on the third rule with the reactive fact gives us that cedar_alkene is bioactive. This completes the logical progression from the original structural fact to the target property.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">
<text>Combining the reactivity with the third rule yields that cedar_alkene is bioactive.</text>
<logic>is_bioactive(cedar_alkene)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Cedar_alkene is is bioactive, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: Bruce is known to be persistent. Whenever someone is principled, they are also empathetic, so if Bruce is principled then Bruce is empathetic. If a person is precise, that guarantees they are resilient; thus precise Bruce would be resilient. On the other hand, being attentive forces a person to lack resilience, meaning an attentive Bruce would not be resilient. Regarding his habits, Bruce is either attentive or studious (or possibly both). In addition, either Bruce is not empathetic or he is precise. Finally, either Bruce is not persistent or he is principled.
examples.question: Is Bruce studious?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are given several facts about Bruce together with conditional statements and alternatives. Our goal is to determine whether the property 'studious' holds for Bruce based on the supplied information.

The first rule tells us that principledness guarantees empathy. This will become useful later when we manage to establish that Bruce is principled, allowing us to derive his empathy.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Bruce is principled, then Bruce is empathetic.</text>
<logic>empathetic(bruce) :- principled(bruce)</logic>
</step>

The second rule links precision to resilience. Once we know Bruce is precise, we can immediately infer his resilience, which will be needed for later contradictions.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Bruce is precise, then Bruce is resilient.</text>
<logic>resilient(bruce) :- precise(bruce)</logic>
</step>

The third rule says that attentiveness forces a lack of resilience. This creates a tension with the previous rule and will help us eliminate the possibility that Bruce is attentive.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Bruce is attentive, then Bruce is not resilient.</text>
<logic>~resilient(bruce) :- attentive(bruce)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction states that Bruce must be either attentive or studious. This provides a direct route to proving he is studious once we can rule out attentiveness.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Bruce is either attentive or studious.</text>
<logic>attentive(bruce) v studious(bruce)</logic>
</step>

The fourth disjunction offers two options: either Bruce is not empathetic or he is precise. When we later learn Bruce is empathetic, the only way to satisfy this statement is to accept that he is precise.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Either Bruce is not empathetic or Bruce is precise.</text>
<logic>~empathetic(bruce) v precise(bruce)</logic>
</step>

The fifth disjunction says Bruce is either not persistent or principled. Since we already know he is persistent, the only way for the statement to hold is that he must be principled.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Either Bruce is not persistent or Bruce is principled.</text>
<logic>~persistent(bruce) v principled(bruce)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Bruce is persistent is a starting point for applying the previous disjunction about persistence and principledness.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Bruce is persistent.</text>
<logic>persistent(bruce)</logic>
</step>

Because Bruce is persistent, the clause "not persistent or principled" can only be true if the second part holds, so we infer that Bruce is principled. This step uses disjunctive syllogism to eliminate the impossible "not persistent" option.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,6">
<text>From Bruce being persistent and the disjunction that either he is not persistent or principled, we infer that Bruce must be principled.</text>
<logic>principled(bruce)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established Bruce is principled, the rule that principled implies empathetic lets us conclude Bruce is empathetic. This is a straightforward application of modus ponens.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,1">
<text>Since Bruce is now known to be principled, the rule that principled implies empathetic lets us conclude Bruce is empathetic.</text>
<logic>empathetic(bruce)</logic>
</step>

We have Bruce as empathetic, and the disjunction "not empathetic or precise" must be satisfied. Since the first alternative is false, the second must be true, giving us Bruce is precise. This again uses disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,5">
<text>Given Bruce is empathetic and the disjunction that either he is not empathetic or precise, we can deduce that Bruce is precise.</text>
<logic>precise(bruce)</logic>
</step>

Having derived that Bruce is precise, we can apply the rule linking precision to resilience, yielding that Bruce is resilient. This follows directly by modus ponens.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,2">
<text>Because Bruce is precise, the rule linking precise to resilient tells us Bruce is resilient.</text>
<logic>resilient(bruce)</logic>
</step>

We now know Bruce is resilient, but the rule "if attentive then not resilient" tells us that an attentive Bruce could not be resilient. Since Bruce is resilient, the antecedent (attentive) must be false, so we infer that Bruce is not attentive. This is a classic contrapositive reasoning (modus tollens).
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,3">
<text>Bruce is resilient, but the rule stating that an attentive person cannot be resilient forces the conclusion that Bruce is not attentive.</text>
<logic>~attentive(bruce)</logic>
</step>

Finally, the original disjunction said Bruce is either attentive or studious. We have just shown he is not attentive, so the only remaining possibility is that Bruce is studious. This again uses disjunctive syllogism to eliminate the first option.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,4">
<text>With Bruce not attentive and the earlier disjunction that he is either attentive or studious, we must conclude that Bruce is studious.</text>
<logic>studious(bruce)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Bruce is studious, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: Alex is clear-headed, and Rio is also clear-headed. Whenever Rio is clear-headed, Rio is adaptable. If Rio is insightful, then Rio is energetic. Being resourceful guarantees that Rio is insightful. Whenever Rio is adaptable, Rio becomes resourceful.
examples.question: Is Rio energetic?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are given a small network of personality traits for two designers, Alex and Rio. Our goal is to determine whether Rio possesses the energetic trait based on the supplied facts and conditional rules.

The first rule tells us that if Rio is clear-headed, then Rio must also be adaptable. This connection will let us move from the known fact about Rio's clarity of mind toward a new property we can later use.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule that Rio being clear-headed implies Rio is adaptable.</text>
<logic>adaptable(rio) :- clear_headed(rio)</logic>
</step>

The second rule states that Rio being insightful guarantees Rio is energetic. It provides the final link in the chain we need: once we can show Rio is insightful, we will automatically have Rio energetic.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule that Rio being insightful implies Rio is energetic.</text>
<logic>energetic(rio) :- insightful(rio)</logic>
</step>

The third rule connects resourcefulness to insightfulness for Rio, saying that a resourceful Rio is automatically insightful. This will help us bridge from resourcefulness—derived earlier—to the insight needed for energy.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule that Rio being resourceful implies Rio is insightful.</text>
<logic>insightful(rio) :- resourceful(rio)</logic>
</step>

The fourth rule says that an adaptable Rio becomes resourceful. This creates a loop: clear-headed leads to adaptable, which then leads to resourceful, setting up the path toward insight and energy.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The rule that Rio being adaptable implies Rio is resourceful.</text>
<logic>resourceful(rio) :- adaptable(rio)</logic>
</step>

We are told that Alex is clear-headed, which is a factual statement but not directly used in the derivation for Rio. It simply mirrors the same property that Rio shares.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Fact: Alex is clear-headed.</text>
<logic>clear_headed(alex)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Rio is clear-headed is crucial because it activates the first rule, allowing us to infer Rio's adaptability.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Fact: Rio is clear-headed.</text>
<logic>clear_headed(rio)</logic>
</step>

Applying the rule from PREMISE_1 to the fact in PREMISE_6, we conclude Rio is adaptable. This step translates the known clear-headedness of Rio into a new trait that will be useful later.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,1">
<text>From Rio being clear-headed and the rule linking clear-headed to adaptable, we infer that Rio is adaptable.</text>
<logic>adaptable(rio)</logic>
</step>

With Rio now known to be adaptable, we invoke the rule from PREMISE_4, which tells us adaptable implies resourceful. Thus we can assert Rio is resourceful, moving one step closer to insight.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,4">
<text>Using the fact that Rio is adaptable and the rule that adaptable leads to resourceful, we conclude Rio is resourceful.</text>
<logic>resourceful(rio)</logic>
</step>

Now that Rio is resourceful, we use the rule from PREMISE_3, which says a resourceful Rio is insightful. This gives us the insight needed to trigger the energetic rule.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,3">
<text>Given Rio is resourceful and the rule that resourceful leads to insightful, we deduce Rio is insightful.</text>
<logic>insightful(rio)</logic>
</step>

Finally, using the insight we just obtained and the rule from PREMISE_2, we deduce Rio is energetic. This completes the chain of implications from the original fact to the desired conclusion.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,2">
<text>Since Rio is insightful and the rule that insightful leads to energetic, we derive that Rio is energetic.</text>
<logic>energetic(rio)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Rio is energetic, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: In the geological survey, the basalt layer is well stratified. The sandstone stratum is well stratified. The limestone formation is well stratified. The shale deposit is well stratified. Whenever a formation is high pressure, it becomes rapid forming. If a formation is rapid forming, then it is not well stratified.
examples.question: Based on the information provided, can we conclude that the shale deposit is analytical?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are given a set of geological properties about several formations and two conditional rules linking high pressure, rapid formation, and stratification. Our goal is to see whether these premises allow us to infer that the shale deposit possesses the analytical property.

I need to determine whether Shale_deposit is analytical can be derived from the given premises.

The first rule tells us that high pressure guarantees rapid formation for the shale deposit. This connection will be useful if we ever learn that the shale deposit is high pressure, because it would let us derive rapid formation.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If the shale deposit is high pressure, then it is rapid forming.</text>
<logic>rapid_forming(shale_deposit) :- high_pressure(shale_deposit)</logic>
</step>

The second rule states that rapid formation leads to a lack of well‑stratification for the shale deposit. Should we manage to infer rapid formation, this rule would let us conclude the opposite of a fact we already have, creating a potential conflict.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If the shale deposit is rapid forming, then it is not well stratified.</text>
<logic>~well_stratified(shale_deposit) :- rapid_forming(shale_deposit)</logic>
</step>

The basalt layer being well stratified is a straightforward fact that does not directly involve the shale deposit, but it establishes the baseline truth of the well‑stratified predicate in the domain.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The basalt layer is well stratified.</text>
<logic>well_stratified(basalt_layer)</logic>
</step>

Similarly, the sandstone stratum is well stratified, reinforcing that the well‑stratified property holds for multiple formations and confirming the consistency of the factual base.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The sandstone stratum is well stratified.</text>
<logic>well_stratified(sandstone_stratum)</logic>
</step>

The limestone formation is also well stratified, further supporting the pattern that all listed formations share this attribute, which will be relevant when we compare it to the rule that can produce a non‑well‑stratified outcome.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The limestone formation is well stratified.</text>
<logic>well_stratified(limestone_formation)</logic>
</step>

The shale deposit is explicitly stated to be well stratified. This fact directly clashes with the potential conclusion of the second rule, meaning we would need evidence of rapid formation to generate a contradiction, but we have no such evidence.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>The shale deposit is well stratified.</text>
<logic>well_stratified(shale_deposit)</logic>
</step>

<step n="7" type="derived">
<text>Searching for rules that conclude Shale_deposit is analytical: no rule or disjunction has "analytical" as a consequent.</text>
<logic>unreachable(analytical(shale_deposit))</logic>
</step>

<step n="8" type="derived">
<text>Similarly, no rule concludes Shale_deposit is not analytical.</text>
<logic>unreachable(~analytical(shale_deposit))</logic>
</step>

<step n="9" type="derived">
<text>Neither Shale_deposit is analytical nor Shale_deposit is not analytical can be derived from the given premises. The answer is Unknown.</text>
<logic>unknown(analytical(shale_deposit))</logic>
</step>

</think>
Answer: Unknown

examples.context: In the ongoing civil engineering effort, the bridge project is known to be deadline driven. Whenever the bridge project employs a flexible design, it is not deadline driven. The bridge project is either flexible in its design or well planned. No other defaults or exceptions are mentioned.
examples.question: Is the bridge project well planned?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether the bridge project is well planned. Our goal is to use the supplied facts and logical relationships to reach a conclusion about the project's planning status.

The conditional rule connects flexible design with not being deadline driven, so it will help us relate the project's known deadline-driven status to its design flexibility. Recognizing this link is essential for later deductions.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if the bridge project has a flexible design, then it cannot be deadline driven.</text>
<logic>~deadline_driven(bridge_project) :- flexible_design(bridge_project)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction tells us that at least one of the two properties—flexible design or well‑planned—must hold for the bridge project. This alternative will become crucial once we eliminate one of the options.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The project is either flexible in its design or well planned.</text>
<logic>flexible_design(bridge_project) v well_planned(bridge_project)</logic>
</step>

The factual statement that the bridge project is deadline driven provides a concrete anchor for applying the conditional rule. It gives us a truth we can compare against the rule's consequent.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>It is given that the bridge project is deadline driven.</text>
<logic>deadline_driven(bridge_project)</logic>
</step>

Given that the project is deadline driven, the rule "if flexible then not deadline driven" would be violated if the project were flexible. To avoid this contradiction, we must conclude the project is not flexible in its design. This is a classic use of modus tollens: the antecedent must be false because the consequent is false while the overall implication holds.
<step n="4" type="derived" from="3,1">
<text>Because the project is deadline driven and a flexible design would make it not deadline driven, we infer that the project is not flexible in its design.</text>
<logic>~flexible_design(bridge_project)</logic>
</step>

Now we know the project is not flexible, and the earlier disjunction says it is either flexible or well planned. Since the flexible option is ruled out, the only remaining possibility is that the project is well planned. This follows the pattern of disjunctive syllogism, where eliminating one disjunct forces acceptance of the other.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>Since the project is not flexible and it must be either flexible or well planned, we conclude that the project is well planned.</text>
<logic>well_planned(bridge_project)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Bridge_project is well planned, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: Hector is insightful. Whenever Hector is judicious, he is also scholarly. If Hector is experienced, then he is not scholarly. Either Hector is not insightful, or he is well prepared. Either Hector is not well prepared, or he is judicious.
examples.question: Can we conclude that Hector is not experienced?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked whether Hector can be shown to lack the property of being experienced. To answer this, we will use the supplied facts, conditional rules, and disjunctions to build a chain of logical deductions leading to the desired negation.

The first premise tells us that judiciousness guarantees scholarly status for Hector. This connection will become useful later when we manage to prove that Hector is judicious, allowing us to derive his scholarly nature.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule that any judicious Hector is also scholarly.</text>
<logic>scholarly(hector) :- judicious(hector)</logic>
</step>

The second premise states that if Hector were experienced, he would necessarily be not scholarly. This contrapositive relationship is crucial because once we establish that Hector is scholarly, we can immediately conclude that he cannot be experienced.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule that any experienced Hector cannot be scholarly.</text>
<logic>~scholarly(hector) :- experienced(hector)</logic>
</step>

The third premise offers a choice: either Hector lacks insightfulness or he is well prepared. Since we already know Hector is insightful, the first alternative is false, forcing the second alternative to hold.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The disjunction that either Hector is not insightful or he is well prepared.</text>
<logic>~insightful(hector) v well_prepared(hector)</logic>
</step>

The fourth premise provides another binary option: either Hector is not well prepared or he is judicious. After we determine that he is well prepared, the negation of the first option becomes impossible, leaving judiciousness as the only viable option.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The disjunction that either Hector is not well prepared or he is judicious.</text>
<logic>~well_prepared(hector) v judicious(hector)</logic>
</step>

We are explicitly told that Hector possesses the quality of being insightful. This fact will interact with the first disjunction to allow us to infer a new property about Hector.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>It is given that Hector is insightful.</text>
<logic>insightful(hector)</logic>
</step>

Because Hector is insightful, the clause "Hector is not insightful" in the first disjunction cannot be true. Disjunctive syllogism therefore lets us discard that side and accept the remaining option, concluding that Hector is well prepared.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,3">
<text>From Hector being insightful and the first disjunction, we infer that Hector must be well prepared.</text>
<logic>well_prepared(hector)</logic>
</step>

Having established that Hector is well prepared, we turn to the second disjunction. The statement "Hector is not well prepared" is now false, so disjunctive syllogism again forces the other side to be true, giving us that Hector is judicious.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,4">
<text>Using the fact that Hector is well prepared together with the second disjunction, we deduce that Hector is judicious.</text>
<logic>judicious(hector)</logic>
</step>

With Hector confirmed as judicious, we can apply the conditional rule from Premise 1. Modus ponens lets us infer that a judicious Hector must also be scholarly, so we derive that Hector is scholarly.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,1">
<text>Since Hector is judicious, the earlier rule gives that he is scholarly.</text>
<logic>scholarly(hector)</logic>
</step>

We now have Hector as scholarly, which directly conflicts with the consequence of the second premise that an experienced Hector would be not scholarly. Using modus tollens, the truth of the scholarly conclusion forces the negation of the antecedent, leading us to conclude that Hector is not experienced.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,2">
<text>Given that Hector is scholarly and the rule linking experienced to not scholarly, we infer that Hector cannot be experienced.</text>
<logic>~experienced(hector)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Hector is not experienced, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

The rule stating that a training_avid loom operator is not pattern_intuitive introduces a potential conflict with the pattern_intuitive we already derived. However, we have no information about the loom operator being training_avid, so the rule does not yet produce a concrete conclusion.

examples.context: In the library, Thea is known to be reflective, while Neil has a reputation for being witty. The library policy states that whenever someone is meticulous, that person is also determined, so if Thea were meticulous she would be determined. It also says that any rational person becomes enthusiastic, which means that if Neil were rational he would be enthusiastic. Another rule declares that reflective individuals are honest, therefore Thea's reflectiveness guarantees her honesty. Additionally, the policy notes that generous people lose their wittiness, so if Neil were generous he would not be witty. The staff also remembers two alternative possibilities: Neil is either generous or rational, and either Thea is not honest or Thea is meticulous. No other default rules apply.
examples.question: Based on the information provided, can we determine whether Neil is adaptable?

<logic>witty(neil)</logic>
</step>

examples.context: Quinoa is rich in protein. Whenever a food is nutrient dense, it also has a balanced nutrient profile. If a food is rich in protein, then it provides an energy boost. examples.question: Is it true that quinoa is not digestible?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to decide whether the statement that quinoa is not digestible holds. To answer this, we will use the supplied facts, conditional rules, and the given disjunction.

The first rule connects nutrient density to a balanced nutrient profile. Recognizing this link will later allow us to move from a property we derive (nutrient density) to the balanced profile needed for the disjunction.

<logic>balanced_nutrient_profile(quinoa) :- nutrient_dense(quinoa)</logic>
</step>

The second rule tells us that richness in protein guarantees an energy‑boosting effect. This will let us translate the explicit fact about quinoa's protein content into a new property we can chain forward.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if something is rich in protein, then it provides an energy boost.</text>
<logic>energy_boosting(quinoa) :- rich_in_protein(quinoa)</logic>

The third rule links the energy‑boosting property back to nutrient density. By establishing that quinoa is energy boosting, we can subsequently infer that it is nutrient dense, which is crucial for applying the first rule.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if something provides an energy boost, then it is nutrient dense.</text>
<logic>nutrient_dense(quinoa) :- energy_boosting(quinoa)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction presents two alternatives: either quinoa lacks a balanced nutrient profile, or it is digestible. Once we can confirm the balanced profile, the only way for the disjunction to stay true is for quinoa to be digestible.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The disjunction says that either quinoa does not have a balanced nutrient profile, or it is digestible.</text>
<logic>~balanced_nutrient_profile(quinoa) v digestibility(quinoa)</logic>
</step>

The fact states directly that quinoa is rich in protein. This concrete piece of information is the starting point for applying the conditional rules that follow.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>We are told directly that quinoa is rich in protein.</text>
<logic>rich_in_protein(quinoa)</logic>
</step>

Because we know quinoa is rich in protein and the rule says richness in protein leads to an energy boost, we can conclude that quinoa provides an energy boost. This step transforms the factual protein content into a dynamic nutritional effect.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,2">
<text>From quinoa being rich in protein and the rule linking richness in protein to energy boosting, we infer that quinoa is energy boosting.</text>
<logic>energy_boosting(quinoa)</logic>
</step>

Having established that quinoa is energy boosting, we now apply the rule that energy‑boosting foods are nutrient dense. This gives us the nutrient‑dense status of quinoa, a key intermediate property for the next inference.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">

<logic>nutrient_dense(quinoa)</logic>
</step>

With quinoa identified as nutrient dense, we invoke the first rule, which asserts that nutrient‑dense foods have a balanced nutrient profile. Consequently, quinoa possesses a balanced nutrient profile, satisfying the left side of the disjunction.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,1">
<text>Given quinoa is nutrient dense and the rule that nutrient dense foods have a balanced nutrient profile, we infer that quinoa has a balanced nutrient profile.</text>
<logic>balanced_nutrient_profile(quinoa)</logic>
</step>

We now have quinoa's balanced nutrient profile, and the disjunction tells us that either quinoa lacks this profile or it is digestible. Since the first alternative is false, the only remaining way for the disjunction to hold is for quinoa to be digestible. Thus, we infer that quinoa is digestible.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,4">

We derived Quinoa is digestibility, so the conclusion Quinoa is not digestibility is False.

<logic>clever(player_morgan) :- hardworking(player_morgan)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction offers two possibilities: either player_morgan is not inventive or player_morgan is driven. When we later confirm that player_morgan is indeed inventive, the first option becomes impossible, forcing the second option—player_morgan being driven.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The disjunction tells us that either player_morgan is not inventive or player_morgan is driven.</text>
<logic>~inventive(player_morgan) v driven(player_morgan)</logic>
</step>

The fact that player_morgan is courteous is the starting point for applying the courtesy‑to‑inventive rule. It gives us a concrete property we can use immediately in the derivation process.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>It is a fact that player_morgan is courteous.</text>
<logic>courteous(player_morgan)</logic>
</step>

From the fact that player_morgan is courteous and the rule that courtesy implies inventiveness, we conclude that player_morgan is inventive. This step uses straightforward modus ponens: the antecedent (courteous) is true, so the consequent (inventive) follows.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">
<text>Because player_morgan is courteous, we can infer that player_morgan is inventive.</text>
<logic>inventive(player_morgan)</logic>
</step>

Having established that player_morgan is inventive, we look back at the disjunction "not inventive or driven". Since the "not inventive" alternative is now false, the only way for the disjunction to remain true is for player_morgan to be driven. This is a classic use of disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,5">
<text>Since player_morgan is inventive, the alternative "not inventive" is false, so the disjunction forces player_morgan to be driven.</text>
<logic>driven(player_morgan)</logic>
</step>

With player_morgan now known to be driven, we can apply the rule that driven implies hardworking. By modus ponens again, the truth of the antecedent (driven) gives us the consequent (hardworking).
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,1">
<text>Given that player_morgan is driven, the rule that driven implies hardworking lets us conclude player_morgan is hardworking.</text>
<logic>hardworking(player_morgan)</logic>
</step>

Now that player_morgan is hardworking, the rule linking hardworking to cleverness allows us to infer that player_morgan is clever. This step continues the chain of modus ponens, moving us closer to logical status.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,4">
<text>Because player_morgan is hardworking, the rule that hardworking implies clever yields that player_morgan is clever.</text>
<logic>clever(player_morgan)</logic>
</step>

Since player_morgan is clever, the rule that cleverness guarantees logical reasoning lets us conclude that player_morgan is logical. This final application of modus ponens provides the property needed to evaluate the query.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,2">
<text>Since player_morgan is clever, the rule that clever implies logical gives that player_morgan is logical.</text>
<logic>logical(player_morgan)</logic>
</step>

We derived Player_morgan is logical, so the conclusion Player_morgan is not logical is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: Ari is known to be persistent. Whenever something is persistent, it is also articulate, so Ari’s persistence guarantees that Ari is articulate. Any articulate individual is automatically curious, which means Ari becomes curious as a result of being articulate. Being curious leads to efficiency, so Ari is efficient once Ari is curious. If someone is generous, then that person is experienced; thus generosity would make Ari experienced. Cheerfulness prevents efficiency, so any cheerful person is not efficient. Finally, either Ari is cheerful or Ari is generous; at least one of these two possibilities holds.
examples.question: Based on the information given, can we conclude that Ari is experienced?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked whether Ari can be shown to be experienced using the supplied facts and rules. The goal is to link the known persistent fact to the experienced conclusion through the chain of conditionals and the disjunction about cheerfulness or generosity. We'll proceed step by step, applying each relevant premise.

This premise gives us the first conditional link in the chain: persistence leads to articulateness. It is crucial because it connects the only concrete fact we have—Ari's persistence—to a new property that can be further exploited.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Ari is persistent, then Ari is articulate.</text>
<logic>articulate(ari) :- persistent(ari)</logic>
</step>

The second premise tells us that articulateness guarantees curiosity. It will allow us to move from the property we derive in the previous step (articulate) to another property (curious) that brings us closer to efficiency.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Ari is articulate, then Ari is curious.</text>
<logic>curious(ari) :- articulate(ari)</logic>
</step>

Here we learn that curiosity implies efficiency. Once we have curiosity, this rule will let us obtain efficiency, an important predicate for the later contradiction with cheerfulness.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Ari is curious, then Ari is efficient.</text>
<logic>efficient(ari) :- curious(ari)</logic>
</step>

This rule connects generosity to experience. It is the final bridge we need: if we can establish that Ari is generous, we will immediately be able to conclude that Ari is experienced.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Ari is generous, then Ari is experienced.</text>
<logic>experienced(ari) :- generous(ari)</logic>
</step>

The fifth premise states that cheerfulness forces a lack of efficiency. This negative relationship will be used later to rule out cheerfulness, thereby activating the disjunction.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Ari is cheerful, then Ari is not efficient.</text>
<logic>~efficient(ari) :- cheerful(ari)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction provides two possibilities: Ari is either cheerful or generous. By eliminating one option, we can force the other, which is essential for reaching the experienced conclusion.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Either Ari is cheerful or Ari is generous.</text>
<logic>cheerful(ari) v generous(ari)</logic>
</step>

The only explicit fact we start with is Ari's persistence. This fact is the foundation on which the entire derivation builds, as it triggers the first rule.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Ari is persistent.</text>
<logic>persistent(ari)</logic>
</step>

Using the fact that Ari is persistent together with the first rule, we can directly infer that Ari is articulate. This is a straightforward application of modus ponens: the condition of the rule is satisfied, so the conclusion follows.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,1">
<text>From Ari’s persistence and the first rule, we infer that Ari is articulate.</text>
<logic>articulate(ari)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know Ari is articulate, the second rule allows us to deduce curiosity. Again, this follows by modus ponens because the antecedent of the rule (articulate) holds for Ari.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,2">
<text>Using Ari’s articulateness and the second rule, we infer that Ari is curious.</text>
<logic>curious(ari)</logic>
</step>

With curiosity established, the third rule gives us efficiency. This step continues the chain of forward reasoning, each link relying on the property just derived.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,3">
<text>Applying the third rule to Ari’s curiosity gives us that Ari is efficient.</text>
<logic>efficient(ari)</logic>
</step>

We have derived that Ari is efficient, but the fifth rule says a cheerful Ari would be not efficient. Since Ari is efficient, the antecedent "cheerful" must be false; otherwise we would have a contradiction. Hence we conclude Ari is not cheerful, using modus tollens.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,5">
<text>Since Ari is efficient and the fourth rule tells us that cheerful people are not efficient, we conclude that Ari cannot be cheerful.</text>
<logic>~cheerful(ari)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction tells us that Ari is either cheerful or generous. Because we have just shown Ari cannot be cheerful, the only remaining option is that Ari must be generous. This is an application of disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,6">
<text>Given that Ari is not cheerful and that either Ari is cheerful or generous, we deduce that Ari must be generous.</text>
<logic>generous(ari)</logic>
</step>

Having established that Ari is generous, the fourth rule immediately yields that Ari is experienced. This final modus ponens step bridges the generosity we proved to the target predicate experienced.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,4">
<text>Because Ari is generous, the fourth rule lets us infer that Ari is experienced.</text>
<logic>experienced(ari)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Ari is experienced, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

The crucial fact is that River Side is high density. This is the starting point for the specific conditional rules that apply to River Side.

With River Side established as regulated, the third rule immediately yields that River Side is pedestrian_friendly. This completes the chain of reasoning that started from the high density fact.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,3">

We derived River_side is pedestrian friendly, so the conclusion River_side is not pedestrian friendly is False.

These statements together form the complete set of information we have about the router.
examples.question: Is the router monitored?

The first rule tells us that any router that is adaptive will also be monitored. This connection will allow us to move from adaptive to monitored later in the proof.

<text>The rule that any adaptive router is also monitored.</text>
<logic>monitored(router) :- adaptive(router)</logic>
</step>

The second rule states that if a router is encrypted, then it must be adaptive. This will let us derive adaptiveness once we know the router is encrypted.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule that any encrypted router is adaptive.</text>
<logic>adaptive(router) :- encrypted(router)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction says that either the router is not critical or it is encrypted. This gives us a way to infer encryption when combined with knowledge about the router's critical status.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The statement that either the router is not critical or it is encrypted.</text>
<logic>~critical(router) v encrypted(router)</logic>
</step>

The fact we have is that the router is critical. This fact will interact with the disjunction to force a conclusion about encryption.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The fact that the router is critical.</text>
<logic>critical(router)</logic>
</step>

We know from the fact that the router is critical, which directly contradicts the first part of the disjunction (the router is not critical). Because that alternative is false, the only way for the disjunction to hold is for the second alternative— that the router is encrypted—to be true. Hence we conclude the router is encrypted.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,3">
<text>From the router being critical and the disjunction, we infer that the router is encrypted.</text>
<logic>encrypted(router)</logic>
</step>

Having established that the router is encrypted, we can apply the second rule which says encryption implies adaptiveness. Therefore, the router must be adaptive.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,2">
<text>Since the router is encrypted, the rule that encrypted implies adaptive lets us conclude the router is adaptive.</text>
<logic>adaptive(router)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have shown the router is adaptive, we invoke the first rule that adaptive routers are monitored. This leads directly to the conclusion that the router is monitored.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,1">
<text>Because the router is adaptive, the rule that adaptive implies monitored lets us infer the router is monitored.</text>

</step>

Therefore Router is monitored, so the conclusion is True.

examples.context: George is prepared. Whenever something is prepared, it is also cheerful. Whenever something is cheerful, it is observant. If something is skillful, then it is perceptive. If something is polite, then it is not perceptive. Either George is not observant or George is skillful.
examples.question: Is George polite?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether George satisfies the property of being polite. To answer this, we will follow the chain of given facts, rules, and the disjunction, deriving new information until we can evaluate George's politeness.

The first rule tells us that preparedness guarantees cheerfulness. Because we already know George is prepared, this rule becomes immediately relevant: it will allow us to turn the known fact into a new property, cheerfulness, which is needed for later steps.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If something is prepared, then it is cheerful; this rule will let us turn George's preparedness into cheerfulness.</text>
<logic>cheerful(george) :- prepared(george)</logic>
</step>

The second rule links cheerfulness to observance. Once we have established George's cheerfulness, this rule will let us infer that George is observant, moving us closer to using the disjunction that involves observance.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If something is cheerful, then it is observant; we will later use this to obtain George's observance from his cheerfulness.</text>
<logic>observant(george) :- cheerful(george)</logic>
</step>

The third rule connects skillfulness with perceptiveness. Later, when we manage to prove that George is skillful, this rule will let us derive that he is perceptive, a crucial step for attacking the politeness rule.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If something is skillful, then it is perceptive; this connects skillfulness to perceptiveness for George.</text>
<logic>perceptive(george) :- skillful(george)</logic>
</step>

The fourth rule says that if something is polite then it cannot be perceptive. This contrapositive relationship will be used after we have shown George is perceptive; it will let us conclude that George cannot be polite.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If something is polite, then it is not perceptive; this gives a way to infer the negation of politeness from perceptiveness.</text>
<logic>~perceptive(george) :- polite(george)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction states that either George is not observant or he is skillful. Once we have established George's observance, the first part of the disjunction will be false, forcing the second part—skillfulness—to be true.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Either George is not observant or George is skillful; this disjunction will let us derive skillfulness once we know George is observant.</text>
<logic>~observant(george) v skillful(george)</logic>
</step>

The fact that George is prepared is our starting point. It provides the concrete evidence needed to activate the first rule and begin the chain of deductions.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>George is prepared; this is the starting fact about George.</text>
<logic>prepared(george)</logic>
</step>

Applying the rule from PREMISE_1 to the fact from PREMISE_6, we see that a prepared entity must be cheerful, so George inherits cheerfulness. This new fact will be the premise for the next rule.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,1">
<text>From George being prepared and the rule that prepared things are cheerful, we conclude that George is cheerful.</text>
<logic>cheerful(george)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know George is cheerful, we can invoke the rule from PREMISE_2: any cheerful thing is observant. Thus George becomes observant, giving us the necessary piece to evaluate the disjunction.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,2">
<text>From George being cheerful and the rule that cheerful things are observant, we infer that George is observant.</text>
<logic>observant(george)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction from PREMISE_5 offers two possibilities: either George is not observant or he is skillful. Since we have just proved George is observant, the first alternative is ruled out, leaving skillfulness as the only viable option. Hence we infer that George is skillful.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,5">
<text>Because George is observant and the disjunction "not observant or skillful" holds, we can deduce that George must be skillful.</text>
<logic>skillful(george)</logic>
</step>

With George's skillfulness established, we turn to the rule in PREMISE_3, which tells us that skillful entities are perceptive. Consequently, George is perceptive, setting up the condition needed to challenge any claim of politeness.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,3">
<text>Using George's skillfulness and the rule that skillful things are perceptive, we obtain that George is perceptive.</text>
<logic>perceptive(george)</logic>
</step>

We now have George as perceptive, but PREMISE_4 says that if something were polite, it would have to be not perceptive. Because George is perceptive, the antecedent of that conditional cannot hold, leading us to conclude that George is not polite.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,4">
<text>Since George is perceptive and the rule that politeness implies not perceptive, we infer that George cannot be polite.</text>
<logic>~polite(george)</logic>
</step>

We derived George is not polite, so the conclusion George is polite is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: Ember is nutrition aware. Whenever Ember has balanced nutrients, she is high in energy. If Ember is nutrition aware, then she practices mindful eating. The fourth rule provides a negative relationship: if Ember practices portion control, she cannot be mindful about eating. This creates a potential conflict with the earlier derivation of mindful_eating, depending on which side of the disjunction holds.

examples.context: In the kitchen, the chef notes that Onyx, Sasha, and Wren are all insightful cooks. Whenever a cook is insightful, that cook is also principled. If a cook is principled, then the cook is logical. Logical cooks are always compassionate. Compassionate cooks inevitably become sharp. On the other hand, if a cook is methodical, that cook cannot be sharp. Finally, the chef observes that Wren is either methodical or adaptable, but not both at the same time. These observations capture all the facts, rules, and the single disjunction that will be used to reason about Wren's adaptability.
examples.question: Based on the given information, is Wren adaptable?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether Wren possesses the adaptable quality, using the supplied facts and logical relationships. The goal is to chain the given rules together until we can directly answer the query about Wren's adaptability.

<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Wren is logical, then Wren is compassionate.</text>
<logic>compassionate(wren) :- logical(wren)</logic>
</step>

<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Wren is principled, then Wren is logical.</text>
<logic>logical(wren) :- principled(wren)</logic>
</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Wren is insightful, then Wren is principled.</text>
<logic>principled(wren) :- insightful(wren)</logic>
</step>

<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If Wren is compassionate, then Wren is sharp.</text>
<logic>sharp(wren) :- compassionate(wren)</logic>
</step>

<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If Wren is methodical, then Wren is not sharp.</text>
<logic>~sharp(wren) :- methodical(wren)</logic>
</step>

<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Wren is either methodical or adaptable.</text>
<logic>methodical(wren) v adaptable(wren)</logic>
</step>

<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Onyx is insightful.</text>
<logic>insightful(onyx)</logic>
</step>

<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>Sasha is insightful.</text>
<logic>insightful(sasha)</logic>
</step>

<step n="9" type="premise">
<text>Wren is insightful.</text>
<logic>insightful(wren)</logic>
</step>

Wren is explicitly stated to be insightful. The rule that every insightful individual is principled applies universally, so we can instantiate it for Wren. By applying this rule, we safely infer that Wren is principled, establishing the first link in the chain.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,3">
<text>From Wren being insightful and the rule that every insightful cook is principled, we infer that Wren is principled.</text>
<logic>principled(wren)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know Wren is principled, we turn to the rule connecting principled status to logical status. The rule says any principled cook is logical, so with Wren's principled property we can directly derive that Wren is logical. This moves us one step closer to the compassionate condition we need later.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,2">
<text>Because Wren is now known to be principled and the rule says a principled cook is logical, we conclude that Wren is logical.</text>
<logic>logical(wren)</logic>
</step>

Having established Wren's logical nature, we use the rule that logical cooks are compassionate. The implication is straightforward: because Wren is logical, the compassionate property follows automatically. This gives us the compassionate fact required for the next deduction.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,1">
<text>Given that Wren is logical and that logical cooks are compassionate, we deduce that Wren is compassionate.</text>
<logic>compassionate(wren)</logic>
</step>

With Wren now known to be compassionate, we apply the rule that compassionate cooks become sharp. The rule is a direct implication, so from Wren's compassion we deduce that Wren is sharp. This sharpness will be crucial for disproving the methodical alternative.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,4">
<text>Since Wren is compassionate and compassionate cooks become sharp, we obtain that Wren is sharp.</text>
<logic>sharp(wren)</logic>
</step>

The rule about methodical cooks states that if someone is methodical, they cannot be sharp. Since we have proven Wren is sharp, the antecedent of that rule must be false; otherwise we would have a contradiction. Therefore, we conclude that Wren is not methodical, using modus tollens.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,5">
<text>We have that if Wren were methodical, he would not be sharp; because Wren is sharp, we can infer that Wren is not methodical.</text>
<logic>~methodical(wren)</logic>
</step>

We are left with the original disjunction that Wren is either methodical or adaptable. Because we have just shown Wren is not methodical, the only remaining possibility in the disjunction is that Wren is adaptable. This application of disjunctive syllogism yields the desired adaptable property.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,6">
<text>With the knowledge that Wren is not methodical and the earlier statement that Wren is either methodical or adaptable, we conclude that Wren must be adaptable.</text>
<logic>adaptable(wren)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Wren is adaptable, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: The moon is known to be geologically active. Any body that is geologically active is also in a stable orbit. Regarding the moon, either it does not have a stable orbit or it is mission ready.
examples.question: Is the moon not mission ready?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked whether the moon fails to be mission ready. To answer this, we will use the given facts and logical relationships.

The rule tells us that any geologically active body is also in a stable orbit. Since this applies to the moon, we keep it in mind for later derivations.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If the moon is geologically active, then it is in a stable orbit.</text>
<logic>stable_orbit(moon) :- geologically_active(moon)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction states that for the moon, either it does not have a stable orbit or it is mission ready. This provides two possible ways to satisfy the statement, which we will resolve using other information.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>Either the moon is not in a stable orbit, or it is mission ready.</text>
<logic>~stable_orbit(moon) v mission_ready(moon)</logic>
</step>

We are directly told that the moon is geologically active. This concrete fact will allow us to activate the conditional rule.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The moon is geologically active.</text>
<logic>geologically_active(moon)</logic>
</step>

From the fact that the moon is geologically active and the rule that geologically active implies stable orbit, we can conclude the moon is in a stable orbit. This uses modus ponens, linking the specific fact to the general implication.
<step n="4" type="derived" from="3,1">
<text>Therefore, the moon is in a stable orbit.</text>
<logic>stable_orbit(moon)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know the moon has a stable orbit, the left side of the disjunction (~stable_orbit) is false, leaving the only way for the disjunction to hold is that the moon is mission ready. Thus, we deduce the moon is mission ready via disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>Given the stable orbit and the earlier disjunction, we infer that the moon is mission ready.</text>
<logic>mission_ready(moon)</logic>
</step>

We derived Moon is mission ready, so the conclusion Moon is not mission ready is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: The workbench is precise. Whenever something is precise, it is also steady. If something is steady, then it is careful. Either the workbench is not careful, or it is persistent.
examples.question: Is the workbench not persistent?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are told several properties about a workbench and how those properties relate. Our goal is to determine whether the statement that the workbench is not persistent holds true.

The rule linking steadiness to carefulness will later allow us to move from a steady condition to a careful one. This connection is essential for applying the disjunction that involves carefulness.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If the workbench is steady, then it is careful.</text>
<logic>careful(workbench) :- steady(workbench)</logic>
</step>

The link from precision to steadiness provides the bridge from the given fact about precision to the property of steadiness. We will need this to start the chain of deductions.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If the workbench is precise, then it is steady.</text>
<logic>steady(workbench) :- precise(workbench)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction gives us two alternatives: either the workbench lacks carefulness, or it possesses persistence. Once we establish carefulness, the first alternative becomes impossible, forcing the second.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Either the workbench is not careful, or it is persistent.</text>
<logic>~careful(workbench) v persistent(workbench)</logic>
</step>

The fact that the workbench is precise is our starting point. It directly activates the rule that converts precision into steadiness.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The workbench is precise.</text>
<logic>precise(workbench)</logic>
</step>

Applying the rule that precision leads to steadiness, and using the fact that the workbench is precise, we can infer that the workbench is steady. This step converts the known fact into a new property needed for later steps.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>Since the workbench is precise and precision implies steadiness, we conclude the workbench is steady.</text>
<logic>steady(workbench)</logic>
</step>

With the workbench now known to be steady, we invoke the rule that steadiness yields carefulness. Thus we obtain that the workbench is careful, setting up the condition required for the disjunctive reasoning.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>Given the workbench is steady and steadiness implies carefulness, we infer the workbench is careful.</text>
<logic>careful(workbench)</logic>
</step>

We have established that the workbench is careful, which contradicts the first part of the disjunction "not careful". Consequently, the only way for the disjunction to remain true is that the workbench is persistent, so we deduce persistence.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">
<text>Because the workbench is careful and we have that either it is not careful or it is persistent, we deduce it must be persistent.</text>
<logic>persistent(workbench)</logic>
</step>

We derived Workbench is persistent, so the conclusion Workbench is not persistent is False.
</think>
Answer: False
